topics.title: Creation of High-End Part Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL508 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Creation of High-End Part Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**High-End Part Modeling** in the context of Computer Aided Design (CAD) refers to the systematic application of advanced parametric, surface, and feature-based modeling techniques to construct complex mechanical components that cannot be efficiently produced using basic primitive operations (extrude, revolve, cut, fillet, chamfer). It involves the use of multi-body modeling, advanced sweeps and lofts, freeform surfaces (Bezier, B-Spline, NURBS), sheet metal features, weldment structures, and configuration-driven parametric design, all governed by a fully constrained and dimension-driven feature tree.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A *High-End Part Model* is defined as a fully parametric 3D solid model in which the design intent is captured through a robust feature tree, advanced surfaces, and engineering features such that any change in the input parameters automatically propagates through the entire model geometry (associative parametric behavior).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **sculptor working with intelligent clay**:

- **Basic Modeling (Low-End)** is like making a clay ball and squishing it with your hands — simple shapes only.
- **High-End Part Modeling** is like using a programmable robotic arm that follows a **blueprint (sketch)**, a **recipe (feature tree)**, and a **list of ingredients (parameters)**. If you change the recipe — say, the size of an ingredient — the entire sculpture automatically reshapes itself, just like a smart 3D model.

In a CAD environment, the "blueprint" is the **2D Sketch**, the "recipe" is the **Feature Manager Design Tree**, and the "ingredients" are the **Dimensions, Equations, and Global Variables**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Design Philosophy:** The Three Pillars of High-End Modeling
> 1. **Parametric** — Driven by dimensions and equations
> 2. **Associative** — Features update automatically with parent changes
> 3. **Intent-Driven** — Captures *why* a design is shaped that way (not just *what* it looks like)

### 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU Lab Records

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Parametric Modeling** | A modeling approach where dimensions and constraints control geometry. |
| **Feature Tree** | Chronological list of operations applied to construct a 3D part. |
| **Design Intent** | The logical reasoning behind a design's geometry to support future modifications. |
| **NURBS** | Non-Uniform Rational B-Splines — mathematical representation of curves and surfaces. |
| **Loft** | A feature that creates a solid by blending profiles through multiple cross-sections. |
| **Sweep** | A feature that creates a solid by moving a profile along a path. |
| **Multi-Body** | Multiple independent solid bodies within a single part file. |
| **Configuration** | A variation of a part created by changing dimensions or suppressing features. |
| **Surface Modeling** | Construction of zero-thickness faces used to define complex external geometry. |
| **Sheet Metal** | Specialized modeling for parts manufactured by bending flat sheet stock. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard CAD Software Benchmarks (as per KTU Lab Infrastructure):**
> Students are expected to be proficient in at least one of: **SolidWorks**, **CATIA V5**, **Siemens NX**, or **PTC Creo Parametric**. The KTU lab typically deploys **SolidWorks** for evaluation.

---

### 1.4 Classification of High-End Part Models

High-end parts are generally classified into the following engineering families, each demanding a distinct modeling strategy:

1. **Cast / Forged Components** — Engine blocks, pump housings (require draft analysis, fillet chains).
2. **Sheet Metal Assemblies** — Panels, brackets, enclosures (use specialized sheet metal tools).
3. **Plastic Injection Molded Parts** — Mobile phone casings, bottle caps (require draft, ribs, bosses).
4. **Weldment Structures** — Frames, machine bases (use weldment profiles, cut lists).
5. **Aesthetic / Freeform Parts** — Consumer product casings, automotive interiors (use surface modeling).
6. **Functional Mechanism Parts** — Gears, cams, springs (use equations, configurations, patterns).

---

### 1.5 Why High-End Modeling Matters (Industry Relevance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Statistic (per industry survey references):**
> Over **80%** of product design time is spent in the **conceptual and detailed CAD modeling** phase. High-end modeling reduces rework, accelerates design changes, and supports downstream CAE (Computer Aided Engineering) and CAM (Computer Aided Manufacturing) workflows.

The transition from a 2D concept to a high-end 3D part is the **single most important skill** for any mechanical/CAD engineer, as it directly impacts:
- DFMEA (Design Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)
- Tolerance stack-up analysis
- Manufacturing process selection
- Cost estimation
- Finite Element Analysis (FEA) mesh quality

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Anatomy of a Feature Tree

A high-end part is not a single 3D blob — it is a **chronological assembly of features**, each dependent on the previous. The feature tree (FeatureManager Design Tree in SolidWorks) is the DNA of the part.

A robust feature tree follows these principles:

- **Origin-based sketches:** All base sketches should be drawn on the Front, Top, or Right plane, fully constrained to the **Origin (0,0,0)**.
- **Minimum feature count:** Use the fewest features that fully define the geometry. Avoid creating geometry that will be removed by later cuts.
- **Logical naming:** Rename generic features (e.g., "Boss-Extrude1") to descriptive names (e.g., "Bearing_Pocket_Cut").
- **Parent-child awareness:** Understand that child features depend on parent features. Deleting a parent deletes all children.

### 2.2 Sketching Fundamentals for High-End Parts

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Examination Tip:**
> Most marks in the lab exam are awarded for **sketch quality**, not just the final 3D shape.

A **fully defined** sketch is one in which all lines, arcs, and splines are dimensioned and constrained such that the sketch cannot be moved or resized. This is the foundation of a parametric part.

**Sketch Entities Used in High-End Modeling:**

- Lines, Circles, Arcs, Ellipses
- Splines (for freeform curves)
- Construction Geometry (reference lines and points, shown in blue)
- Trim, Extend, Offset, Mirror
- 3D Sketches (for sweep paths along non-planar trajectories)

**Sketch Relations (Geometric Constraints):**

| Relation | Symbol | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Horizontal | H | Forces line to be horizontal |
| Vertical | V | Forces line to be vertical |
| Coincident | ⊙ | Connects two points |
| Concentric | ◎ | Aligns arcs/circles to a common center |
| Equal | $=$ | Sets two entities to equal length/radius |
| Tangent | T | Connects an arc and line smoothly |
| Symmetric | → | Mirrors entities about a centerline |
| Fix | ⛓ | Locks geometry in place |

### 2.3 Advanced Feature Operations

#### 2.3.1 Sweep Feature

A **sweep** creates a solid by moving a **profile (cross-section)** along a **path (trajectory)**.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

The swept surface is generated by translating a 2D profile curve $P(u)$ along a 3D path curve $C(t)$:

$$
S(u, t) = C(t) + R(t) \cdot P(u)
$$

where $R(t)$ is the rotation matrix (or Frenet-Serret frame) that orients the profile perpendicular to the path at parameter $t$.

**Types of Sweeps:**
1. **Simple Sweep** — Profile remains normal to the path.
2. **Guide Curve Sweep** — Profile follows additional guide curves to control twist.
3. **Twist Along Path** — Profile rotates by a specified angle along the path.
4. **Tapered Sweep** — Profile scales linearly along the path.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule:** The profile must **not** intersect the path, and the path must be a single, continuous, non-self-intersecting curve for the sweep to succeed.

#### 2.3.2 Loft Feature

A **loft** creates a solid or surface by blending between **two or more cross-sectional profiles** placed in parallel (or non-parallel) planes.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

For two profiles $P_1(u)$ and $P_2(u)$ at parameters $t=0$ and $t=1$, the lofted surface is:

$$
S(u, v) = (1 - v) \cdot P_1(u) + v \cdot P_2(u), \quad v \in [0, 1]
$$

For more than two profiles, multi-section lofting uses higher-order blending (typically cubic B-spline interpolation).

**Key Loft Options in High-End Modeling:**
- **Start/End Constraints:** None, Perpendicular to profile, Tangent to face, Direction Vector.
- **Guide Curves:** Force the loft to follow user-defined curves.
- **Centerline:** Used for closed-shape lofts to control internal geometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Common Exam Question:** *"Explain the difference between a Sweep and a Loft feature with suitable examples."*

#### 2.3.3 Boundary Surfaces and Freeform Surfacing

For aesthetic parts, **surface modeling** is preferred over solid modeling. The core operations are:

- **Boundary Surface** — Creates a surface patch from 2, 3, or 4 boundary edges.
- **Filled Surface** — Creates a surface within a closed boundary.
- **Lofted Surface, Swept Surface** — Surface versions of loft and sweep.
- **Knit Surface** — Combines multiple surfaces into a single connected surface body.
- **Thicken** — Converts a zero-thickness surface into a solid by adding material on one or both sides.
- **Trim Surface, Extend Surface, Untrim Surface** — Surface editing operations.

**Mathematical Foundation — NURBS:**

A NURBS curve of degree $p$ is defined as:

$$
C(u) = \frac{\sum_{i=0}^{n} N_{i,p}(u) \cdot w_i \cdot P_i}{\sum_{i=0}^{n} N_{i,p}(u) \cdot w_i}
$$

where:
- $P_i$ = Control points (i = 0 to n)
- $w_i$ = Weights
- $N_{i,p}(u)$ = B-spline basis functions
- $u$ = Parameter varying along the curve

> The NURBS formulation allows representation of conic sections (circles, ellipses) and freeform curves using a single unified mathematical framework — this is why **ISO 10303 (STEP)** uses NURBS for CAD data exchange.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Operation | Key Formula / Concept | Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Parametric Equation of a Line in Sketch | $y = mx + c$ | mm | Used in dimension-driven sketches |
| Arc Length for Sweep Path | $L = R \cdot \theta$ | mm | $R$ = radius, $\theta$ = angle in radians |
| NURBS Curve | $C(u) = \frac{\sum N_{i,p}(u) w_i P_i}{\sum N_{i,p}(u) w_i}$ | — | Degree $p$, weights $w_i$ |
| Loft Blend Parameter | $S(u,v) = (1-v)P_1(u) + vP_2(u)$ | — | Linear blend; cubic for smoother |
| Bezier Curve (degree 3) | $B(t) = (1-t)^3 P_0 + 3(1-t)^2 t P_1 + 3(1-t) t^2 P_2 + t^3 P_3$ | — | $t \in [0,1]$ |
| Surface Area (general) | $A = \iint_S dS$ | mm² | Used in FEA pre-processing |
| Volume of a Lofted Body | $V = \int_V dV$ (numerically computed) | mm³ | CAD tools compute via B-rep |
| Sheet Metal Bend Allowance | $BA = \frac{\pi \theta}{180} (R + K \cdot T)$ | mm | $R$ = inside bend radius, $T$ = thickness, $K$ = K-factor (typically **0.33** for soft materials, **0.50** for hard) |
| Bend Deduction | $BD = 2 \cdot (\text{outside setback}) - BA$ | mm | Used in flat pattern development |
| Springback Allowance (Sheet Metal) | $\theta_{compensated} = \theta_{nominal} + \Delta\theta$ | degrees | Empirical correction |
| Pattern Spacing (Linear) | $S = n \cdot d$ | mm | $n$ = number of instances, $d$ = spacing |
| Pattern Angle (Circular) | $\alpha = \frac{360^\circ}{N}$ | degrees | $N$ = number of instances |
| Gear Module (Spur Gear) | $m = \frac{d}{z}$ | mm | $d$ = pitch diameter, $z$ = number of teeth |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants to Memorize for KTU Lab Viva:**
> - Standard K-factor for steel sheet metal: **0.44**
> - Standard sheet metal bend radius (default): **1.0 × thickness**
> - Standard global tolerance: **±0.1 mm** (general), **±0.01 mm** (precision)
> - ISO 128 drawing standard for technical drawings

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Loft** is used in designing impellers, propellers, aircraft winglets, and bottle shapes.
- **Sweep** is used in designing springs, pipes, cables, and architectural moldings.
- **NURBS Surfacing** is the industry standard for automotive body panels (BMW, Tesla, Tata Motors).
- **Sheet Metal** is essential for HVAC ducts, electronics enclosures, automotive panels.
- **Parametric Equations** drive gear trains, cam profiles, and mechanism synthesis in CAD.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: NURBS Basis Function (Cox-de Boor Recursion)

The NURBS curve is built upon the B-spline basis function, which is defined recursively. Understanding this derivation is critical for KTU viva questions on surface modeling.

**Step 1 — Define the Knot Vector**

A knot vector is a non-decreasing sequence of real numbers:

$$
U = \{u_0, u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_{n+p+1}\}
$$

For example, for a degree-2 (quadratic) NURBS with 4 control points, the uniform knot vector is:

$$
U = \{0, 0, 0, 1, 2, 3, 3, 3\}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the Basis Function of Degree 0**

$$
N_{i,0}(u) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } u_i \leq u < u_{i+1} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

This is a simple "step function" that is 1 within a single knot interval and 0 elsewhere.

**Step 3 — Recursive Definition (Cox-de Boor Recursion)**

$$
N_{i,p}(u) = \frac{u - u_i}{u_{i+p} - u_i} N_{i,p-1}(u) + \frac{u_{i+p+1} - u}{u_{i+p+1} - u_{i+1}} N_{i+1,p-1}(u)
$$

**Explanation of Logic:**

The recursive formula combines two lower-degree basis functions, weighted by a ratio of parameter distances. This ensures the basis functions are smooth, continuous, and partition unity (they always sum to 1 at any $u$).

**Step 4 — Construct the NURBS Curve**

$$
C(u) = \frac{\sum_{i=0}^{n} N_{i,p}(u) \cdot w_i \cdot P_i}{\sum_{i=0}^{n} N_{i,p}(u) \cdot w_i}
$$

**Explanation of Logic:**

- The numerator computes a weighted sum of control points, where the weights are the product of basis function value $N_{i,p}(u)$ and the user-defined weight $w_i$.
- The denominator is a normalization factor that ensures $C(u)$ is a true point in 3D space (rational, not polynomial).
- A weight $w_i > 1$ pulls the curve **toward** control point $P_i$.
- A weight $w_i < 1$ pushes the curve **away** from $P_i$.
- Setting all $w_i = 1$ reduces NURBS to a standard B-spline.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Viva Favourite Question:** *"Why do we use NURBS instead of simple B-splines in CAD?"*
> **Answer:** NURBS can exactly represent conic sections (circles, ellipses, parabolas) — which B-splines cannot. This is critical for CAD/CAM data exchange (STEP, IGES formats).

### 3.2 Derivation: Sheet Metal Bend Allowance

The **bend allowance (BA)** is the arc length of the neutral axis of the material as it bends through angle $\theta$.

**Step 1 — Locate the Neutral Axis**

When a sheet of thickness $T$ is bent with inside radius $R$, the neutral axis (the line that neither stretches nor compresses) lies at a distance $K \cdot T$ from the inside surface, where $K$ is the **K-factor**.

The radius of the neutral axis is:

$$
R_{neutral} = R + K \cdot T
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Arc Length**

For a bend through angle $\theta$ (in radians), the arc length is:

$$
BA = R_{neutral} \cdot \theta = (R + K \cdot T) \cdot \theta
$$

**Step 3 — Convert Degrees to Radians**

$$
\theta_{rad} = \theta_{deg} \cdot \frac{\pi}{180^\circ}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Formula**

$$
BA = (R + K \cdot T) \cdot \frac{\pi \theta_{deg}}{180^\circ}
$$

**Numerical Example for KTU Lab:**

Given:
- Sheet thickness $T = 2$ mm
- Inside bend radius $R = 3$ mm
- Bend angle $\theta = 90^\circ$
- K-factor $K = 0.44$ (mild steel)

**Step-by-Step Calculation:**

$$
R_{neutral} = 3 + (0.44 \times 2) = 3 + 0.88 = 3.88 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\theta_{rad} = 90 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{\pi}{2} \approx 1.5708 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
BA = 3.88 \times 1.5708 \approx 6.095 \text{ mm}
$$

> The flat pattern of the sheet metal part must allow an extra **6.095 mm** of material at the bend location to compensate for material deformation.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Feature Selection Guide

| Design Requirement | Recommended Feature | Why? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Uniform cross-section along a curved path | **Sweep** | Profile remains constant. |
| Variable cross-section between two profiles | **Loft** | Blends between profiles. |
| Complex organic shape (car body panel) | **Surface Modeling (NURBS)** | Provides aesthetic control. |
| Sheet metal enclosure | **Sheet Metal Tools** | Handles bend, flat pattern, K-factor. |
| Welding frame structure | **Weldment** | Generates cut list and weld beads. |
| Repeated feature (bolt holes on flange) | **Circular / Linear Pattern** | Parametrically duplicates feature. |
| Symmetric part (right and left halves) | **Mirror** | Reduces modeling effort by 50%. |
| Rotationally symmetric part (shaft, bottle) | **Revolve** | Single sketch revolution. |
| Two-rail sweep (handle, rail) | **Sweep with Guide Curves** | Profile follows two rails. |
| Variable pitch spring | **Sweep with variable pitch** | Uses equations to control pitch. |

### 3.4 Derivation: Parametric Linear Pattern Spacing

For a **linear pattern** with $n$ instances evenly spaced over distance $L$:

$$
\text{Spacing} = \frac{L}{n - 1}
$$

**Example:**

Creating **6 bolt holes** equally spaced over a flange length of **150 mm**:

$$
\text{Spacing} = \frac{150}{6 - 1} = \frac{150}{5} = 30 \text{ mm}
$$

Each successive instance is placed 30 mm apart. If the user changes $L$ to 200 mm, the spacing automatically recomputes to **40 mm**, demonstrating **associative parametric behavior**.

### 3.5 Derivation: Gear Module and Pitch Diameter

For a **spur gear** with $z$ teeth and module $m$:

$$
m = \frac{d}{z} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d = m \cdot z
$$

**Example:**

A gear with **24 teeth** and module **2 mm**:

$$
d = 2 \times 24 = 48 \text{ mm}
$$

The **addendum** (tooth height above pitch circle) is:

$$
a = m = 2 \text{ mm}
$$

The **dedendum** (tooth depth below pitch circle) is:

$$
b = 1.25 \cdot m = 2.5 \text{ mm}
$$

The **outside diameter** is:

$$
d_o = d + 2a = m(z + 2) = 2(24 + 2) = 52 \text{ mm}
$$

The **root diameter** is:

$$
d_r = d - 2b = m(z - 2.5) = 2(24 - 2.5) = 43 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Tip:** When modeling a gear in SolidWorks, use **Equations** to link the tooth count, module, and pitch diameter. The whole gear family (e.g., 12T, 24T, 36T) can then be generated using **Configurations** without redrawing.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-End Part Modeling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Analyze Engineering Drawing] --> B[Identify Design Intent]
    B --> C[Create Base Sketch on Origin Plane]
    C --> D{Fully Constrain Sketch?}
    D -- No --> E[Add Dimensions and Relations]
    E --> D
    D -- Yes --> F[Choose Base Feature Type]
    F --> G[Extrude or Revolve]
    G --> H[Add Advanced Features]
    H --> I[Sweep Loft or Surface]
    I --> J[Apply Patterns and Mirrors]
    J --> K[Add Fillets and Chamfers]
    K --> L[Apply Material and Appearance]
    L --> M[Add Configurations]
    M --> N[Validate with Section View]
    N --> O[Export STEP or DXF]
    O --> P[Final High-End Part Model]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Explanation of Flow:** The workflow is a closed loop where each decision feeds into the next. Skipping the "Fully Constrain Sketch" step (D) leads to undefined geometry, which is the **#1 cause** of model failure in KTU lab exams.

### 4.2 Feature Tree Architecture for a High-End Part

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Origin] --> B[Base Sketch Front Plane]
    B --> C[Base Extrude]
    C --> D[Sketch on Top Face]
    D --> E[Pocket Cut]
    E --> F[Sketch on Side Face]
    F --> G[Revolve Cut]
    G --> H[Fillet Edges]
    H --> I[Linear Pattern Holes]
    I --> J[Shell Command]
    J --> K[Material Steel]
    K --> L[Configuration Heavy Duty]
    L --> M[Final Part Body]
```

> The arrows represent **parent-child dependencies**. Removing node D (sketch) will cascade-delete E, I, J, and all subsequent features.

### 4.3 Sweep vs Loft vs Revolve — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[SWEEP]
        S1A[Profile Cross Section] --> S1B[Path Trajectory]
        S1B --> S1C[Swept Solid]
    end
    subgraph S2[LOFT]
        S2A[Profile 1] --> S2C[Blended Solid]
        S2B[Profile 2] --> S2C
        S2D[Profile 3 Optional] --> S2C
    end
    subgraph S3[REVOLVE]
        S3A[2D Sketch] --> S3B[Axis of Revolution]
        S3B --> S3C[Revolved Solid]
    end
```

### 4.4 Sheet Metal Design Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Select Base Flange Tab] --> B[Choose Sheet Thickness and Material]
    B --> C[Sketch Base Profile]
    C --> D[Apply Edge Flanges]
    D --> E[Add Hem and Bend Features]
    E --> F[Add Relief Cuts]
    F --> G[Create Flat Pattern]
    G --> H[Export DXF for Laser Cutting]
    H --> I[Generate Cost Estimation Report]
```

### 4.5 Parametric Equation Linking in CAD

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Global Variable D1 Length] --> B[Extrude Depth]
    A --> C[Linear Pattern Spacing]
    A --> D[Fillet Radius]
    E[Global Variable D2 Width] --> F[Pocket Width]
    E --> G[Mirror Offset]
    B --> H[Final Geometry]
    C --> H
    D --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
```

> **Engineering Insight:** By linking all dimensions to **2 global variables** (D1 and D2), the entire part can be scaled or re-sized by editing just two values — a hallmark of high-end parametric design.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Design Intent" in the context of high-end CAD modeling. Why is it important?**

**Model Answer:**

Design Intent refers to the logical reasoning and design strategy embedded in a 3D model that ensures the part behaves correctly when dimensions or features are modified. It captures *why* a feature is shaped in a particular way, not just *what* it looks like.

**Importance:**
1. Enables robust parametric modification without breaking the model.
2. Facilitates design reuse across product families.
3. Reduces rework during engineering change orders (ECOs).
4. Supports downstream CAE and CAM operations.

Example: Drawing a slot dimensioned from the **center of a part** rather than from one edge reflects a designer's intent to keep the slot centered during scaling.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse "Design Intent" with "Design Drawing" or "Design Specification." Design Intent is specifically about the **parametric logic** of the model.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a Sweep and a Loft feature in SolidWorks. Give one industrial example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Sweep | Loft |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of Profiles | Single profile | Two or more profiles |
| Path | Required (trajectory) | Optional (uses guide curves) |
| Cross-section | Constant along path | Variable across profiles |
| Best For | Springs, pipes, cables | Bottles, aircraft winglets, impellers |

**Industrial Examples:**
- **Sweep:** Helical compression spring in a vehicle suspension, PVC pipe bend.
- **Loft:** Soap dispenser bottle, aircraft wing-fuselage fairing, ceiling fan blade.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the step-by-step procedure to create a **helical compression spring** as a high-end part model in SolidWorks using the Sweep feature. Include all sketches, equations, and global variables required.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define **NURBS** and derive the mathematical expression for a NURBS curve. State two advantages of NURBS over traditional B-spline curves in CAD applications.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Helical Spring Modeling [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Define Global Variables (Equations Tool)**

Open **Tools → Equations** and add the following global variables:

| Variable | Value | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `WireDia` | $1.5$ mm | Wire diameter |
| `MeanDia` | $20$ mm | Mean coil diameter |
| `NumCoils` | $8$ | Number of active coils |
| `Pitch` | $5$ mm | Distance between consecutive coils |

**Step 2: Create the 3D Helix Path**

1. Go to **Insert → Curve → Helix/Spiral**.
2. Define:
   - Pitch = `Pitch = 5 mm`
   - Revolution = `NumCoils = 8`
   - Height = `NumCoils × Pitch = 40 mm`
3. Click OK to generate the helical curve.

**Step 3: Create the Wire Cross-Section Sketch**

1. Select the **Front Plane** and create a new sketch.
2. Draw a **circle of diameter `WireDia = 1.5 mm`**, with its center coincident with the **Origin**.
3. Fully constrain the sketch.

**Step 4: Apply the Sweep Feature**

1. Go to **Insert → Boss/Base → Sweep**.
2. Select the **circle** as the **Profile**.
3. Select the **helix** as the **Path**.
4. Click OK to generate the helical spring.

**Step 5: Add End Caps (Optional)**

1. Use **Fillet** of radius `WireDia/2 = 0.75 mm` at the start and end of the spring.
2. The result is a fully parametric spring that automatically resizes when global variables are edited.

> **Engineering Validation:** The spring rate $k$ can be computed using:

$$
k = \frac{G \cdot d^4}{8 \cdot D^3 \cdot N}
$$

where $G = 79.3$ GPa (steel), $d$ = wire diameter, $D$ = mean coil diameter, $N$ = number of active coils.

---

#### Part (b) — NURBS Definition and Derivation [7 Marks]

A **Non-Uniform Rational B-Spline (NURBS)** is a mathematical model used to represent curves and surfaces with arbitrary shapes. It generalizes B-splines by allowing non-uniform knot spacing and rational (weighted) basis functions.

**Derivation:**

A NURBS curve of degree $p$ is defined as:

$$
C(u) = \frac{\sum_{i=0}^{n} N_{i,p}(u) \cdot w_i \cdot P_i}{\sum_{i=0}^{n} N_{i,p}(u) \cdot w_i}, \quad u_{\min} \leq u \leq u_{\max}
$$

where:
- $P_i$ = Control points (i = 0, 1, …, n)
- $w_i$ = Weights associated with each control point
- $N_{i,p}(u)$ = B-spline basis functions of degree $p$
- $u$ = Curve parameter

The B-spline basis functions are defined recursively using the **Cox-de Boor** algorithm:

$$
N_{i,0}(u) = \begin{cases} 1 & u_i \leq u < u_{i+1} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

$$
N_{i,p}(u) = \frac{u - u_i}{u_{i+p} - u_i} N_{i,p-1}(u) + \frac{u_{i+p+1} - u}{u_{i+p+1} - u_{i+1}} N_{i+1,p-1}(u)
$$

**Two Advantages of NURBS over B-Splines:**

1. **Exact Conic Representation:** NURBS can exactly represent circles, ellipses, parabolas, and hyperbolas, whereas B-splines can only approximate them. This is critical for CAD data exchange (STEP/IGES files).
2. **Local Control via Weights:** Modifying a single weight $w_i$ changes the curve's shape **locally** near control point $P_i$, providing fine-grained aesthetic control required in automotive and consumer product design.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the **Sheet Metal Design** workflow in SolidWorks. Derive the **Bend Allowance** formula and compute the flat pattern length for an L-bracket with the following specifications:
- Sheet thickness: $T = 3$ mm
- Inside bend radius: $R = 4$ mm
- Bend angle: $\theta = 90^\circ$
- K-factor: $K = 0.44$
- Leg 1 length: $L_1 = 100$ mm
- Leg 2 length: $L_2 = 80$ mm

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is a **Configuration** in SolidWorks? Explain how **Design Tables** are used to manage families of high-end parts. List any three parameters that can be controlled via Design Tables.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Sheet Metal Design [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Sheet Metal Workflow in SolidWorks**

1. Open a new part file.
2. Navigate to the **Sheet Metal** tab (visible only if the Sheet Metal add-in is enabled).
3. Click **Base Flange/Tab**, select a plane, and sketch the base profile.
4. Set **Thickness** = 3 mm, **Bend Radius** = 4 mm, **K-Factor** = 0.44.
5. Use **Edge Flange** to add additional bent walls.
6. Use **Hem** to fold back edges for safety.
7. Use **Bend** to add custom bends.
8. Use **Jog** to create offset bends.
9. Use **Relief Cut** to manage material flow at corners.
10. Click **Flat Pattern** to unfold the part into a 2D layout.
11. Export as DXF for laser cutting or CNC punching.

**Step 2: Derivation of Bend Allowance**

The neutral axis of a bent sheet lies at distance $K \cdot T$ from the inside bend surface. The radius of the neutral axis is:

$$
R_{neutral} = R + K \cdot T
$$

The arc length of the neutral axis for a bend of $\theta$ degrees is:

$$
BA = R_{neutral} \cdot \theta_{rad} = (R + K \cdot T) \cdot \frac{\pi \theta}{180^\circ}
$$

**Step 3: Numerical Computation**

Given: $T = 3$ mm, $R = 4$ mm, $\theta = 90^\circ$, $K = 0.44$

$$
R_{neutral} = 4 + (0.44 \times 3) = 4 + 1.32 = 5.32 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\theta_{rad} = 90 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.5708 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
BA = 5.32 \times 1.5708 = 8.357 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4: Flat Pattern Length**

The flat pattern length is the sum of the two legs minus the bend deduction (which accounts for the material compressed at the bend):

$$
L_{flat} = L_1 + L_2 - BD
$$

where $BD$ is the bend deduction. The bend allowance and deduction are related by:

$$
BD = 2 \cdot \text{Outside Setback} - BA
$$

For the given geometry, the outside setback is:

$$
OSSB = \tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot (R + T) = \tan(45^\circ) \cdot (4 + 3) = 7 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
BD = 2 \times 7 - 8.357 = 14 - 8.357 = 5.643 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L_{flat} = 100 + 80 - 5.643 = 174.357 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final Answer:** The flat pattern length of the L-bracket is **174.36 mm**.

---

#### Part (b) — Configurations and Design Tables [7 Marks]

**Definition:**

A **Configuration** in SolidWorks is a named variation of a part or assembly created by changing dimensions, suppressing features, or modifying component states, all within a **single source file**.

**Design Tables:**

Design Tables are **Excel-based or CSV-based spreadsheets** that drive configurations parametrically. Each row in the spreadsheet defines a unique configuration, and each column controls a parameter (dimension, suppression state, etc.).

**Workflow:**

1. Create the master part with all features.
2. Right-click the part name in the FeatureManager → **Add Configuration** (or **Create Design Table**).
3. For Design Tables, link an Excel sheet containing parameter values for each configuration.
4. Switch between configurations by double-clicking them in the ConfigurationManager.

**Three Parameters Controlled by Design Tables:**

1. **Dimensions** — e.g., length, width, height, hole diameter, fillet radius.
2. **Feature Suppression States** — Show or hide features (e.g., ribs on/off, hole drilled/not).
3. **Custom Properties** — e.g., part number, material, cost, supplier name.

**Example — Gear Family:**

A single SolidWorks file can store a 12-tooth, 18-tooth, 24-tooth, and 36-tooth gear. By switching configurations, the model updates automatically, including all derived dimensions (pitch diameter, addendum, dedendum) computed via **Equations**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Forgetting to **link** the design table properly results in the table becoming detached. Always check **Tools → Options → File Locations → Design Tables** to ensure the file path is valid. If the table is detached, switching configurations will fail silently.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in High-End Part Modeling Exams (per KTU Board Examiner Reports):**
>
> 1. **Un-Defined Sketches (–3 marks):** Submitting sketches with yellow (under-defined) icons. *Always ensure your sketch turns black (fully defined) before exiting.*
> 2. **Wrong Base Plane (–2 marks):** Sketching on a random face instead of the **Front/Top/Right Plane** anchored to the Origin. This breaks the parametric model.
> 3. **Hard-Coded Dimensions (–2 marks):** Using numerical values instead of **Equations/Global Variables**. High-end modeling requires parametric linkage.
> 4. **Missing Section View in Lab Record (–1 mark):** The KTU lab record format requires a **section view** and a **detail view** of the final part. Students often omit these.
> 5. **No Design Intent Justification (–1 mark):** Writing the procedure without explaining *why* a particular sketch dimension is from the center or *why* a fillet radius is $2$ mm.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Bullet List for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Design Intent** captures the *why* behind a model's geometry; it is the cornerstone of high-end modeling.
- A **Fully Defined Sketch** is black in SolidWorks; an **Under-Defined Sketch** is blue (or yellow, depending on version). Always aim for **black**.
- **Sweep** uses 1 profile + 1 path. **Loft** uses 2 or more profiles. **Revolve** uses 1 profile + 1 axis.
- **NURBS** = Non-Uniform Rational B-Spline; it is the **ISO standard** for CAD data exchange (STEP, IGES).
- **Sheet Metal Bend Allowance** $BA = (R + K \cdot T) \cdot \frac{\pi \theta}{180^\circ}$. K-factor is **0.44** for steel, **0.33** for aluminum.
- **Configurations** allow multiple part variants in a single file; **Design Tables** (Excel-driven) automate configuration switching.
- **Equations** link dimensions symbolically (e.g., `D1 = D2 / 2`); they propagate changes across the entire feature tree.
- **Multi-Body Modeling** allows multiple solid bodies in a single part file — useful for weldments, molds, and cast parts.
- **Pattern Types:** Linear, Circular, Mirror, Sketch-Driven, Curve-Driven, Table-Driven. Each has a unique use case.
- **Surface Modeling** (Boundary, Loft, Sweep, Filled) is preferred over solid modeling for aesthetic freeform parts.
- **K-factor** for **mild steel** = **0.44**, for **stainless steel** = **0.45**, for **aluminum** = **0.33**, for **copper** = **0.35**.
- **Fillet Order Matters:** Apply large fillets **first**, then small fillets. Reverse order creates unwanted geometry intersections.
- **Shell Command** hollows a solid body; wall thickness must be less than the smallest feature dimension.
- **Mirrored Bodies** save modeling time for symmetric parts; mirror plane must be a flat datum or origin plane.
- **Draft Analysis** is critical for cast and molded parts; SolidWorks offers a Draft Analysis tool (angle threshold typically **1° to 3°**).
- **Export Formats:** Use **STEP (.step)** for data exchange with other CAD tools; **DXF** for 2D flat patterns; **STL** for 3D printing; **IGES** (legacy) for older systems.
- **GD&T (Geometric Dimensioning and Tolerancing)** per **ASME Y14.5 / ISO 1101** is applied to fully dimensioned engineering drawings.
- **KTU Lab Record Format** (mandatory sections): (1) Aim, (2) Software & System Requirements, (3) Theory, (4) Procedure with CAD screenshots, (5) Step-by-step screenshot narrative, (6) Final 3D view, (7) Section view, (8) Result, (9) Conclusion, (10) Viva questions and answers.
- **Recommended CAD Software for KTU Lab:** SolidWorks (most common), followed by CATIA V5, Siemens NX, and Creo Parametric.
- **Common Viva Questions:** "What is a feature tree?" "Explain the difference between a wireframe, surface, and solid model." "What is the role of a K-factor in sheet metal design?" "How do you create a configuration in SolidWorks?"

# 📘 MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — ASSEMBLY MODELING OF STANDARD MACHINE COMPONENTS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assembly Modeling** is the process of combining multiple individually designed part models into a single virtual product by applying geometric and mechanical **constraints (mates)** that define the relative position, orientation, and motion between the components. In the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course: **PCAUL508**), this experiment focuses on building *bottom-up assemblies* of ten classical machine elements, verifying kinematic correctness, and exporting them for FEA or drafting.

In **SolidWorks / CATIA / Creo / NX / Fusion 360**, an assembly is a `.SLDASM`, `.CATProduct`, `.ASM`, `.PRT` (assembly mode) or `.F3Z` file that references one or more part files and stores a tree of *mates* (constraints).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Skeleton 🦴

> [!NOTE]
> Think of an assembly like a **human skeleton**:
> - **Bones** = individual part files (`.SLDPRT`)
> - **Ligaments & Joints** = mates (Revolute, Rigid, Concentric, Tangent)
> - **Skeleton as a whole** = Assembly file
>
> You cannot "walk" with loose bones; you need joints. Similarly, you cannot "rotate" a shaft and a flange unless you apply a **concentric + coincident** mate — exactly as a hip joint allows a femur to rotate inside a socket.

### 1.3 The Ten Assemblies — A Quick Synopsis

| # | Assembly | Primary Function | Key Mates Used |
|---|----------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Socket & Spigot Joint | Permanent pipe-to-flange connection | Coincident, Concentric |
| 2 | Knuckle Joint | Articulating link in tie-rods / steering | Coincident, Revolute concept |
| 3 | Rigid Flange Coupling | Rigid shaft-to-shaft torque transmission | Concentric, Coincident |
| 4 | Bushed Pin Flexible Coupling | Misalignment-tolerant shaft coupling | Concentric + Pin insertion |
| 5 | Plummer Block | Pillow bearing housing for shafts | Coincident, Distance/Lock |
| 6 | Single Plate Clutch | Torque transmission via friction plate | Concentric, Coincident |
| 7 | Cone Friction Clutch | Higher torque via conical contact | Concentric, Coincident |
| 8 | Pipe Joints | Branched fluid transport | Concentric |
| 9 | Screw Jack | Lifting heavy loads via screw | Concentric, Coincident, Helical concept |
| 10 | Tail Stock | Lathe workpiece support | Concentric, Coincident, Distance |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard metric for fasteners in KTU lab:** Use **M10 / M12 bolts** with **1.5 mm pitch**, **Grade 8.8**, to keep models light and avoid tolerance conflicts. Bolt circle diameter = **2 × pitch circle radius (PCR)**.

# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD SHEET

## 2.1 The Assembly Modeling Pipeline

Every assembly in this experiment follows the same **five-stage pipeline**:

1. **Part Readiness (Pre-Assembly)**
   - Each component is modeled as a *separate* part file.
   - Origin assignment: For *mating convenience*, every part should have its **axis of symmetry coincident with the default origin plane** of the part file.
2. **Insertion into Assembly Environment**
   - `Insert → Component → Existing Part` in SolidWorks.
   - First inserted part is **fixed** by default (grounded).
3. **Application of Mates (Constraints)**
   - Sequential mating: Mate the *most important axis first* (concentricity), then the secondary faces (coincidence/distance).
4. **Degrees-of-Freedom (DOF) Analysis**
   - Each mate removes specific DOFs. A fully constrained body has **0 DOF**. A part with rotational freedom has **1 DOF**.
5. **Verification & Explode View**
   - Drag the assembly to ensure no over- or under-constraint.
   - `Ctrl + drag` to make an exploded view for the lab record.

## 2.2 The "Mate Language" — Universal Across CAD Tools

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners expect you to **name your mates correctly** in the viva. Memorize the six primary mate types.

| Mate Type | Symbolic Meaning | Removes DOF | Typical Use |
|-----------|------------------|--------------|-------------|
| **Coincident** | Faces/lines/points share the same plane/line/point | 2 translational | Flange face-to-face contact |
| **Concentric** | Cylindrical faces share the same axis | 2 translational + 2 rotational | Shaft inside a hub |
| **Distance** | Maintains a fixed gap | 1 translational | Bearing clearance, clutch gap |
| **Angle** | Fixed angular offset | 1 rotational | Bracket positioning |
| **Tangent** | Surfaces touch at one point | 1 translational | Belt on pulley |
| **Parallel / Perpendicular** | Geometric orientation | 2 rotational | Pipe brackets |

### 2.3 Component-Wise Design Philosophy (Why each shape exists)

#### A. Socket & Spigot Joint
- **Spigot** = the *male* projection; **Socket** = the *female* recess.
- Mating logic: Concentric mate aligns the axes; Coincident mate seats the spigot shoulder against the socket flange.
- **Engineering utility**: Cast iron pipe lines carrying water at low pressure (≤ 7 bar).

#### B. Knuckle Joint
- Consists of a **single eye**, **double eye**, a **knuckle pin**, and a **taper pin / split pin**.
- The pin allows **rotation**; therefore the assembly is *kinematic*, not rigid.
- **Formula for pin diameter**:
$$
d = \sqrt{\frac{2.6 \, P}{\tau_{safe}}}
$$
where $P$ is the axial pull and $\tau_{safe}$ is the allowable shear stress (~ $40 \, \text{N/mm}^2$ for mild steel).

#### C. Rigid Flange Coupling
- Two **flanges** keyed to two shafts, bolted together.
- The **bolt circle diameter (BCD)** is critical:
$$
D_{bolt} = D_{shaft} + 2 \times \text{flange\,thickness\,factor}
$$
- Typical BCD = **2.5 d** to **3 d** (where $d$ = shaft diameter).

#### D. Bushed Pin Flexible Coupling
- Two **flanges** with protruding **pins**; mating flanges have **rubber / leather bushes**.
- The **bush** provides misalignment tolerance of $\pm 0.5^{\circ}$ and axial float of $\pm 2 \, \text{mm}$.

#### E. Plummer Block (Pillow Block)
- A **cast iron housing** with a **split bearing** (usually a journal bearing) clamped by **two cap bolts**.
- Two halves are joined at the **horizontal split plane**.

#### F. Single Plate Clutch
- A **friction plate** is pressed against a **flywheel face** by a **pressure plate** via **thrust springs**.
- Number of springs = typically **6 to 12** placed on a circle of diameter:
$$
D_{spring} = \frac{2}{3} D_{clutch}
$$

#### G. Cone Friction Clutch
- **Conical contact** generates axial thrust from torque:
$$
T = \mu \, F_n \, R_m \quad ; \quad F_n = \frac{2 \, T}{\mu \, D_m \, \sin \alpha}
$$
where $\alpha$ is the cone half-angle (typically **12.5°–20°**) and $D_m$ is the mean cone diameter.

#### H. Pipe Joints (Flanged)
- Uses **gaskets** between flanges for leak-proofing. Gasket diameter range:
$$
D_{gasket} = D_{pipe\,OD} + 2 \times \text{gasket\,thickness}
$$

#### I. Screw Jack
- **Lead screw** inside a **nut** fixed in the body. Rotating the screw raises/lowers the **cup (cap)**.
- **Mechanical advantage**:
$$
MA = \frac{\pi \, d_m}{L}
$$
where $L$ is the lead and $d_m$ is the mean thread diameter.

#### J. Tail Stock
- Slides on the lathe bed's **prismatic** guide. The **quill** moves axially within the body.
- Quill travel is typically **100–150 mm** in KTU lab lathes.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Use | Units |
|---|---------|-----|-------|
| 1 | $T = \mu \, F_n \, R_m$ | Friction clutch torque | N·m |
| 2 | $F_n = \dfrac{2T}{\mu D_m \sin\alpha}$ | Cone clutch axial thrust | N |
| 3 | $P = \dfrac{2\pi T \, N}{60}$ | Power transmitted | W |
| 4 | $d_{pin} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2.6 P}{\tau_{safe}}}$ | Knuckle pin diameter | mm |
| 5 | $L_{bolt} = t_{flange1} + t_{gasket} + t_{flange2} + nut$ | Bolt length | mm |
| 6 | $BCD = D_{shaft} + k \cdot d_{bolt}$ | Bolt circle diameter | mm |
| 7 | $M.A. = \dfrac{\pi d_m}{L}$ | Screw jack advantage | — |
| 8 | $F_{lift} = \dfrac{W}{M.A. \cdot \eta}$ | Effort on screw jack | N |
| 9 | $A_{friction} = \pi D_m b$ | Single plate clutch area | mm² |
| 10 | $D_m = D - b \sin\alpha$ | Mean cone diameter | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> $\eta$ for screw jack is typically **0.30 to 0.45** due to high thread friction.

# 🛠️ STEP-BY-STEP MODELING APPROACH — ALL TEN ASSEMBLIES

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following is the **exhaustive step-by-step CAD procedure** for each assembly. Follow it in the order written for guaranteed success in the lab exam.

## 3.1 Pre-Assembly: Naming Convention (Mandatory)

Create a folder `PCAUL508_EXP2_<YourRollNo>` and inside it, sub-folders for each assembly. For every assembly, use this file naming:

```
Socket_Spigot → Socket.SLDPRT, Spigot.SLDPRT, Bolt.SLDPRT, Nut.SLDPRT
Knuckle_Joint → SingleEye.SLDPRT, DoubleEye.SLDPRT, Pin.SLDPRT, TaperPin.SLDPRT
...
```

## 3.2 Procedure — Socket & Spigot Joint

**Step 1: Part Modeling**

- **Spigot**: Sketch a circle $\phi 50$, extrude to $40 \, \text{mm}$. Add a flange ring $\phi 80$ × $10 \, \text{mm}$ at one end.
- **Socket**: Sketch the same circle, extrude to $45 \, \text{mm}$ to accommodate the spigot plus a $5 \, \text{mm}$ recess. Add flange $\phi 80$ × $10 \, \text{mm}$.

**Step 2: Assembly**

- New Assembly → Insert Spigot → Fix it.
- Insert Socket.
- **Mate 1 (Concentric)**: Inner cylindrical face of Socket ↔ Outer cylindrical face of Spigot.
- **Mate 2 (Coincident)**: Flange face of Socket ↔ Flange face of Spigot.
- Insert 4 × M10 bolts: **Concentric** each bolt to a hole, **Coincident** bolt head to flange face.

**Step 3: Verify**
- Try to drag the socket; it should only translate along the axis if bolts are loose, or stay fixed if bolts are added.

## 3.3 Procedure — Knuckle Joint

**Step 1: Parts**
- **Single Eye**: Forged fork with one hole $\phi d$.
- **Double Eye**: Fork with two prongs each having a hole.
- **Knuckle Pin**: Cylinder of diameter $d$, length $\approx 1.5 \times \text{thickness of eye}$.
- **Taper Pin**: Small pin perpendicular to the knuckle pin to lock it.

**Step 2: Assembly**
- Insert Single Eye → Fix.
- Insert Double Eye.
- **Mate 1 (Concentric)**: Knuckle Pin ↔ Eye hole.
- **Mate 2 (Concentric)**: Knuckle Pin ↔ Other eye hole.
- Insert Taper Pin → **Concentric** to cross-hole → **Coincident** to face.

**Step 3: Kinematic Check**
- Rotate the double eye — it should revolve freely around the pin axis. **This is the only assembly in the list that is intentionally non-rigid.**

## 3.4 Procedure — Rigid Flange Coupling

**Parts**: Two identical **Flanges** (hub + disc), **Two Shafts** (driver & driven), **Key**, **Bolts**, **Nuts**.

**Mating Sequence**:
1. Insert Flange 1 → Fix.
2. Insert Shaft 1 → **Concentric** to flange bore → **Coincident** shaft end to flange face.
3. Insert Key into keyway.
4. Insert Flange 2.
5. **Concentric** Flange 2 to Flange 1 (faces touching).
6. **Concentric** each of 4 bolts to its respective hole pair.
7. **Coincident** each bolt head to flange face, nut to opposite flange face.

**Design Tip**: Number of bolts = **4, 6, or 8** depending on torque; use $n = 4$ for KTU lab.

## 3.5 Procedure — Bushed Pin Flexible Coupling

**Parts**: Two **Flanges with pins**, two **bushes (rubber/leather)**, two **Shafts**.

**Mating**:
- Concentric mate pin to bush.
- Coincident mate flange faces (with bushes acting as spacers).
- This assembly permits angular misalignment because bushes are flexible.

## 3.6 Procedure — Plummer Block

**Parts**: **Base**, **Cap**, **Bearing (journal)**, **2 Cap Bolts**, **2 Nuts**, **Foundation Bolts**.

**Mating**:
1. Insert Base → Fix.
2. Insert Bearing → **Concentric** to base bore → **Coincident** to base parting face.
3. Insert Cap → **Coincident** to base parting face.
4. **Concentric** cap bolts to bolt holes (passing through cap and base lugs).
5. **Coincident** bolt head to cap top face, nut under base.

## 3.7 Procedure — Single Plate Clutch

**Parts**: **Flywheel**, **Friction Plate** (with splined hub), **Pressure Plate**, **Springs (6 nos.)**, **Lever pins**.

**Mating**:
- Flywheel fixed to engine shaft (concentric + coincident).
- Friction plate slides axially on splines (concentric only — DOF = 1 translation).
- Pressure plate mates concentric to flywheel, with axial play.

**Note**: The springs are typically shown as a *circular pattern* in SolidWorks — use the **Circular Pattern** feature to instance one spring 6 times.

## 3.8 Procedure — Cone Friction Clutch

**Parts**: **Male cone** (on driving shaft), **Female cone** (on driven shaft), **Spring**, **Operating lever**.

**Critical Sketch**: The cone half-angle $\alpha = 12.5°$ is drawn as a line making that angle with the axis. Use the **Revolve** feature.

**Mating**:
- Concentric male & female cones.
- Coincident faces at the rear (back of male cone touches back of female).
- Distance mate of $\sim 2$–$3 \, \text{mm}$ to represent disengagement.

## 3.9 Procedure — Pipe Joints (Flanged)

**Parts**: **Pipe 1**, **Pipe 2 (with branch)**, **Two Flanges**, **Gasket**, **Bolts & Nuts**.

**Mating**:
- Concentric pipe ends to flange bores.
- Coincident flange faces (with gasket sandwiched).
- Concentric bolts to bolt holes.

> [!TIP]
> Use a **Single Line Font** in drawing mode for pipe centerline marking (chain-dotted line).

## 3.10 Procedure — Screw Jack

**Parts**: **Body** (with integral nut at base), **Lead Screw (spindle)**, **Cup/Cap** (with thrust pad), **Handle bar**.

**Mating**:
1. Insert Body → Fix.
2. Insert Lead Screw.
3. **Concentric** screw to body bore.
4. **Coincident** screw bottom end to the *internal shoulder* of the body's nut.
5. Insert Cup → **Concentric** to screw → **Coincident** to screw top end.
6. Insert Handle → **Concentric** to screw through a cross-hole.

**Verification**: Rotate the handle → cup moves linearly up/down. **This validates the Helical/Screw mate logic** (in SolidWorks, you can simulate this using a "Screw" relationship between two concentric cylinders).

## 3.11 Procedure — Tail Stock

**Parts**: **Base** (with V-groove to fit lathe bed), **Body** (with quill bore), **Quill** (with Morse taper inside), **Hand Wheel**, **Lead Screw**.

**Mating**:
1. Insert Base → Fix.
2. Insert Body → **Coincident** to base top face.
3. Insert Quill → **Concentric** to body bore.
4. **Coincident** quill back end to hand wheel hub.
5. Hand wheel is **Concentric** to lead screw.
6. Lead screw is **Concentric** to a cross-hole in quill.

**Mating Degrees of Freedom**:
- Quill should slide axially and rotate (2 DOFs).
- This is a classic **3D sketch + slider mate** scenario in SolidWorks.

## 3.12 Master Summary of Mating Operations

| Assembly | # of Mates | Tightest Constraint |
|----------|------------|---------------------|
| Socket-Spigot | 6 (1 conc + 1 coinc + 4 bolts) | Fully rigid |
| Knuckle | 4 | 1 rotational DOF |
| Rigid Flange | 9 | Fully rigid |
| Bushed Pin | 8 | Axial + angular float |
| Plummer Block | 9 | Fully rigid |
| Single Plate | 5 + circular pattern | 1 translational DOF |
| Cone Clutch | 4 | 1 translational DOF |
| Pipe Joint | 7 | Fully rigid |
| Screw Jack | 5 | Helical motion (1 DOF) |
| Tail Stock | 6 | 1 rotational + 1 translational |

# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List any six types of mates used in SolidWorks assembly modeling. State the primary use of the *concentric* mate.**

**Model Answer**:
1. Coincident
2. Concentric
3. Distance
4. Angle (or Angle Mate)
5. Tangent
6. Parallel
7. Perpendicular (bonus)

**Concentric mate** is used to align two cylindrical, conical, or spherical faces such that they share a common axis. It removes two translational and two rotational degrees of freedom.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a rigid flange coupling and a bushed pin flexible coupling. State one engineering application of each.**

**Model Answer**:

| Feature | Rigid Flange | Bushed Pin Flexible |
|---------|--------------|---------------------|
| Misalignment | None allowed | Permits angular & axial |
| Component | Two flanges + bolts | Two flanges + rubber bushes + pins |
| Application | Lathe spindles, pumps | IC engine flywheel to gearbox |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the step-by-step procedure to create the assembly model of a **Rigid Flange Coupling** in SolidWorks. List all the parts and mates used.

**Model Answer**:

**Parts Required** (modeled beforehand as individual `.SLDPRT` files):
- Flange 1 (Hub + Disc, with bolt holes)
- Flange 2 (mirror of Flange 1)
- Driver Shaft
- Driven Shaft
- Key (rectangular)
- 4 × M10 Bolts
- 4 × M10 Nuts

**Step-by-Step Procedure**:
1. Open SolidWorks → New → Assembly.
2. **Insert Flange 1** from the file menu → Place at origin → Click ✓ (it is *fixed* by default).
3. **Insert Driver Shaft** → Mate its cylindrical surface **Concentric** with the flange bore. Mate its end face **Coincident** with the flange's front face.
4. **Insert Key** → Concentric to keyway + Coincident to keyway slot.
5. **Insert Flange 2** → Concentric to Flange 1's bolt circle (use outer cylindrical reference) OR just align via the matching face.
6. Apply **Coincident** mate between the two flat disc faces of the flanges.
7. **Insert 4 Bolts** one by one. For each bolt:
   - **Concentric** mate to a bolt-hole pair.
   - **Coincident** mate for the bolt head to Flange 1 face.
   - **Coincident** mate for the nut to Flange 2 face.
8. Insert the Driven Shaft into Flange 2 (mirror of Step 3).
9. Verify: Drag Flange 2 — it should be **fully fixed**.
10. Create an **Exploded View** (`Ctrl + drag` each component radially outward) for the lab record.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, derive the relationship between the **axial thrust $F_n$** and the **torque $T$** transmitted by a **Cone Friction Clutch**.

**Model Solution**:

Consider a cone clutch with half-angle $\alpha$, mean radius $R_m = D_m / 2$, and an axial thrust $F_n$ pressing the male cone into the female cone.

**Step 1 — Normal Force on Elemental Area**:
The elemental normal force on a small ring of radius $r$ and width $dr$ on the cone surface is:
$$
dF_n^{local} = p \cdot (2\pi r \, dr) \cdot \sin\alpha
$$
where $p$ is the uniform contact pressure (in $\text{N/mm}^2$).

**Step 2 — Frictional Torque from the Ring**:
The frictional force on the ring is $\mu \cdot p \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$, and its moment arm is $r$:
$$
dT = \mu \, p \, (2\pi r \, dr) \cdot r = 2\pi \mu \, p \, r^2 \, dr
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate Across the Cone Face**:
Integrating $dT$ from the inner radius $r_1$ to the outer radius $r_2$:
$$
T = 2\pi \mu p \int_{r_1}^{r_2} r^2 \, dr = 2\pi \mu p \left[ \frac{r^3}{3} \right]_{r_1}^{r_2}
$$
$$
T = \frac{2\pi \mu p}{3} \left( r_2^3 - r_1^3 \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Relate Pressure to Axial Thrust**:
The total axial thrust equals the integral of the vertical component of $dF_n^{local}$:
$$
F_n = \int_{r_1}^{r_2} p \cdot 2\pi r \, dr \cdot \sin\alpha = \pi p (r_2^2 - r_1^2) \sin\alpha
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Pressure**:
$$
p = \frac{F_n}{\pi (r_2^2 - r_1^2) \sin\alpha}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Back**:
$$
T = \frac{2\pi \mu}{3} \cdot \frac{F_n}{\pi (r_2^2 - r_1^2) \sin\alpha} \cdot (r_2^3 - r_1^3)
$$
$$
T = \frac{2\mu F_n}{3 \sin\alpha} \cdot \frac{r_2^3 - r_1^3}{r_2^2 - r_1^2}
$$

**Step 7 — Approximation for a Shallow Cone** (when $r_2 \gg r_1$):
$$
\frac{r_2^3 - r_1^3}{r_2^2 - r_1^2} \approx \frac{r_2^3}{r_2^2} = r_2 = \frac{D_m}{2}
$$
where $D_m$ is the **mean cone diameter**.

Thus the **design equation** is:
$$
\boxed{\,T = \frac{\mu F_n D_m}{2 \sin\alpha}\,} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad F_n = \frac{2T}{\mu D_m \sin\alpha}
$$

**Engineering Insight**: Because $\sin 12.5^\circ \approx 0.216$, the axial thrust required is roughly **2.3× higher than the simple flat-plate clutch** for the same torque — but the *self-energizing* effect at higher angles can reduce it.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Model the **assembly of a Screw Jack** in SolidWorks. State the design formula for its **mechanical advantage (M.A.)** and the **effort required** to lift a load of $W = 20 \, \text{kN}$ given mean screw diameter $d_m = 30 \, \text{mm}$ and lead $L = 6 \, \text{mm}$. Assume efficiency $\eta = 0.40$.

**Model Answer**:

**Parts**:
- Body (with vertical cylindrical bore and integral ACME thread at base)
- Lead Screw (spindle) with handle hole at top
- Cup / Cap (with thrust pad and center hole)
- Handle Bar

**Mating Steps**:
1. Insert Body → Fix.
2. Insert Lead Screw → **Concentric** to body bore → **Coincident** bottom of screw to the *top of the internal nut*.
3. Insert Cup → **Concentric** to screw top end → **Coincident** cup base to screw top face.
4. Insert Handle → **Concentric** to screw cross-hole.

**Mechanical Advantage**:
$$
M.A. = \frac{\pi \, d_m}{L} = \frac{\pi \times 30}{6} = 5\pi \approx 15.708
$$

**Effort Required**:
The ideal effort (frictionless) is:
$$
P_{ideal} = \frac{W}{M.A.} = \frac{20{,}000}{15.708} \approx 1273.24 \, \text{N}
$$

Accounting for efficiency:
$$
P_{actual} = \frac{P_{ideal}}{\eta} = \frac{1273.24}{0.40} \approx 3183.1 \, \text{N} \approx 3.18 \, \text{kN}
$$

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat assembly sketch, explain the construction and working of a **Plummer Block**. List the standard parts used.

**Model Answer**:

**Definition**: A Plummer Block (also called a *Pillow Block*) is a pedestal-type bearing housing used to support a rotating shaft, typically at the ends of line shafts in industrial machinery.

**Standard Parts** (refer to the assembly diagram you draw):
1. **Base (Pedestal)** — cast iron, with two lugs for foundation bolts.
2. **Cap (Top Half)** — bolted to base, encloses the bearing.
3. **Bearing (Journal / Brass bush)** — fits between the shaft and the housing.
4. **Cap Bolts (2 nos.)** — clamp the cap to the base.
5. **Nuts and Washers** for cap bolts.
6. **Foundation Bolts (2 nos.)** — fix the block to the floor.
7. **Oil Hole / Grease Nipple** — for lubrication.

**Working**: The shaft is supported inside the brass bearing, which is held within the housing. Lubricant is supplied through the oil hole. The cap is removed for bearing replacement. The split design at the horizontal parting plane allows quick maintenance without dismantling the shaft.

**Mating Strategy in CAD**:
- Concentric bearing to base bore.
- Coincident cap to base parting face.
- Concentric + Coincident cap bolts.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warnings ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common reasons for losing marks in Assembly Modeling exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to "Fix" the first part.** If no part is fixed, the assembly *floats* — and dragging the assembly will move *all* parts, not just the inserted one. KTU examiners **deduct 2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Applying mates in the wrong order.** Always **concentric first, coincident second**. Reverse order causes "redundant mates" and assembly failure.
> 3. **Confusing the knuckle pin's role.** The knuckle joint is the *only* assembly that retains a rotational degree of freedom by design. Examiners *will* mark you down if you over-constrain it.
> 4. **Not creating an exploded view.** A KTU lab record **must** include a labeled exploded view. Missing it = **−3 marks**.
> 5. **Using the wrong bolt size.** Always use **M10 or M12** with **1.5 mm pitch**. M8 is too small for flange assemblies.
> 6. **Omitting the gasket or bush.** In Pipe Joints and Bushed Pin couplings, students often forget the soft component — examiners specifically check for it.
> 7. **Wrong origin placement.** If a part's origin is *not* on the axis of symmetry, the concentric mate will fail or misalign. Always align part origin to the axis *before* modeling the geometry.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

- ✅ Every assembly starts by **fixing the first inserted part** — this is your reference.
- ✅ Apply mates in this priority: **Concentric → Coincident → Distance/Angle → Tangent**.
- ✅ **Knuckle joint is the only non-rigid** assembly in this experiment — preserve its rotational DOF.
- ✅ **Rigid flange coupling** has *two flanges + two shafts + one key + 4 bolts + 4 nuts*.
- ✅ **Bushed pin coupling** has *flexible bushes* — the key feature that makes it "flexible."
- ✅ **Plummer block** always has a *horizontal split plane* between base and cap.
- ✅ **Single plate clutch** uses a *circular pattern* of springs — typically **6 springs** on a circle of diameter $2D/3$.
- ✅ **Cone friction clutch** derives its design from:
$$
T = \frac{\mu F_n D_m}{2 \sin\alpha}
$$
- ✅ **Pipe joints** must have a **gasket** between the flanges.
- ✅ **Screw jack** uses the *Helical motion* principle — rotation converted to translation. Its M.A. is $\pi d_m / L$.
- ✅ **Tail stock** has *two* motion DOFs: the body translates on the lathe bed, and the quill slides inside the body.
- ✅ **Standard bolts**: M10 × 1.5 pitch for all KTU lab assemblies unless specified.
- ✅ **Always create an Exploded View** before exporting the drawing.
- ✅ **Save frequently** — SolidWorks crashes more often than you'd think!
- ✅ **Verify DOF** in the status bar — it should read **0 DOF** for rigid assemblies.
- ✅ **Use metric units** throughout. KTU lab records in imperial units = **automatic 1-mark deduction**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structural Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL508 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Structural Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Structural Analysis** in the context of a Computer-Aided Design and Analysis Lab refers to the systematic application of the **Finite Element Method (FEM)** to predict the mechanical response of engineering components (beams, trusses, frames, plates, and 3-D solids) when subjected to external loads, prescribed displacements, thermal effects, or other environmental conditions. The objective is to determine internal reactions, displacements, stresses, and strains using numerical discretization rather than closed-form analytical solutions.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCAUL508**, this experiment specifically deals with the **static linear elastic structural analysis** of a 1-D or 2-D structural element (cantilever beam, simply supported beam, or truss) using industry-standard FEA solvers (such as ANSYS Workbench, ANSYS Mechanical APDL, ABAQUS/CAE, or SolidWorks Simulation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Students are expected to perform static structural analysis of a standard benchmark problem (commonly a cantilever beam or a truss), apply suitable boundary conditions, mesh the geometry, solve, and **validate the FEA results against classical strength-of-materials (SOM) analytical results**. Reporting of percentage error is mandatory for full marks in the lab record.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Domino Chain Bridge" Analogy**
> Imagine trying to predict the deflection at the tip of a long, flexible diving board when a person stands on its end. Solving this by hand using pure calculus (Euler-Bernoulli beam theory) requires you to write a single equation that holds for *every* tiny particle of the board. For a simple uniform board, this works. But what if the board is made of **three different materials** bonded together, has a **hole in the middle**, and carries a **non-uniform load**? The single-equation approach fails.
>
> **Finite Element Analysis (FEA)** solves this by chopping (meshing) the diving board into thousands of small, simple pieces (elements). For each tiny piece, we assume a simple behavior (e.g., a straight line or a parabola) and stitch all the pieces back together. The collective behavior of all these small simple pieces **approximates** the complex real behavior. This is the essence of structural analysis using computers.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

The following **standard SI units** (used in all KTU lab reports) must be strictly followed:

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Typical Magnitude in Lab |
|----------|--------|---------|--------------------------|
| Length / Displacement | $L$, $u$, $v$ | meter (m) | $10^{-3}$ m to $1$ m |
| Force / Load | $F$, $P$ | Newton (N) | $1$ N to $10^{5}$ N |
| Stress | $\sigma$ | Pascal (Pa) or N/m² | $10^{6}$ Pa (MPa) typical |
| Strain | $\varepsilon$ | dimensionless (m/m) | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | Pascal (Pa) | Steel: $2 \times 10^{11}$ Pa |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\nu$ | dimensionless | Steel: **0.3**, Aluminium: 0.33 |
| Density | $\rho$ | kg/m³ | Steel: **7850 kg/m³** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Conversion for KTU Lab Records:** $1 \text{ MPa} = 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 1 \text{ N/mm}^2$. Most engineering data sheets list $E$ in GPa ($1 \text{ GPa} = 10^9 \text{ Pa}$). Steel: $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$, Aluminium: $E = 70 \text{ GPa}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of the Finite Element Method

The FEA workflow — and therefore any structural analysis software — rests on three mathematical pillars:

1. **Discretization (Meshing):** The continuous domain $\Omega$ is divided into a finite number of sub-domains (elements) $\Omega^e$, such that $\Omega \approx \bigcup_{e=1}^{N_e} \Omega^e$.
2. **Approximation (Shape Functions):** Within each element, the unknown displacement field $\mathbf{u}(x)$ is approximated as a linear combination of simple polynomial basis functions (shape functions $N_i$) multiplied by nodal values $\mathbf{d}_i$:
   $$\mathbf{u}(x) \approx \hat{\mathbf{u}}(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i(x)\,\mathbf{d}_i$$
3. **Assembly and Solution:** Applying the principle of minimum potential energy (or virtual work) yields the **global stiffness equation**:
   $$\mathbf{K}\,\mathbf{U} = \mathbf{F}$$
   where $\mathbf{K}$ is the global stiffness matrix, $\mathbf{U}$ is the nodal displacement vector, and $\mathbf{F}$ is the applied force vector.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step FEA Logic (How the Solver Works)

- **Step 1: Pre-processing.** Geometry is built (or imported). Material properties ($E$, $\nu$, $\rho$) and element type (beam, shell, solid) are assigned.
- **Step 2: Discretization.** The geometry is split into a mesh of elements. For 1-D bar/beam problems, line elements suffice. For 2-D plane stress/strain, triangular (TRI3, TRI6) or quadrilateral (QUAD4, QUAD8) elements are used. For 3-D, tetrahedral (TET4, TET10) or hexahedral (HEX8, HEX20) elements are used.
- **Step 3: Element Stiffness Matrix.** For each element, an element stiffness matrix $\mathbf{k}^e$ is computed by integrating $E$, geometry, and shape function derivatives over the element volume:
  $$\mathbf{k}^e = \int_{V^e} \mathbf{B}^T \mathbf{D} \mathbf{B}\, dV$$
  where $\mathbf{B}$ is the strain-displacement matrix and $\mathbf{D}$ is the material constitutive matrix.
- **Step 4: Assembly.** The global stiffness matrix $\mathbf{K}$ is formed by summing the contributions of all element stiffness matrices using the connectivity (element-node incidence) data:
  $$\mathbf{K} = \bigotimes_{e=1}^{N_e} \mathbf{k}^e$$
- **Step 5: Apply Boundary Conditions.** Prescribed displacements (essential BCs) and loads (natural BCs) are applied. Essential BCs are imposed by modifying rows/columns of $\mathbf{K}$ and corresponding entries of $\mathbf{F}$.
- **Step 6: Solve the Linear System.** Direct (Gauss elimination, LU) or iterative (Conjugate Gradient) solvers compute $\mathbf{U} = \mathbf{K}^{-1}\mathbf{F}$.
- **Step 7: Post-processing.** Strains $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} = \mathbf{B}\mathbf{U}$ and stresses $\boldsymbol{\sigma} = \mathbf{D}\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ are recovered. Nodal averaging is performed for visualization.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Stiffness Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The global stiffness matrix $\mathbf{K}$ is **symmetric**, **positive semi-definite** (positive definite once BCs are applied), and **sparse** (most entries are zero because each node connects to only a few neighbors). This sparsity is what makes modern FEA tractable for millions of unknowns.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table is the **single most important reference** for validating lab results against analytical solutions. Memorize these for the KTU university practical exam viva.

| # | Structural Element | Analytical Formula | Description |
|---|--------------------|---------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Axial bar elongation | $\delta = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$ | $P$: axial load, $L$: length, $A$: cross-section, $E$: Young's modulus |
| 2 | Axial bar stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Uniform along length (Saint-Venant's principle ignored) |
| 3 | Cantilever beam tip deflection (point load at free end) | $\delta_{tip} = \dfrac{PL^3}{3EI}$ | $I$: second moment of area about neutral axis |
| 4 | Cantilever beam tip deflection (UDL $w$ over span) | $\delta_{tip} = \dfrac{wL^4}{8EI}$ | UDL = uniformly distributed load in N/m |
| 5 | Simply supported beam centre deflection (point load at centre) | $\delta_{centre} = \dfrac{PL^3}{48EI}$ | $P$ at mid-span |
| 6 | Simply supported beam centre deflection (UDL over full span) | $\delta_{centre} = \dfrac{5wL^4}{384EI}$ | Most common KTU bench-mark problem |
| 7 | Maximum bending stress in beam | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M_{max} \cdot c}{I} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{Z}$ | $Z = I/c$: section modulus |
| 8 | Second moment of area (rectangular section, $b \times h$) | $I = \dfrac{bh^3}{12}$ | $h$ is dimension in bending direction |
| 9 | Second moment of area (circular section, diameter $d$) | $I = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{64}$ | Solid circular cross-section |
| 10 | Truss element (2-node bar) stiffness in global frame | $k = \dfrac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} c^2 & cs & -c^2 & -cs \\ cs & s^2 & -cs & -s^2 \\ -c^2 & -cs & c^2 & cs \\ -cs & -s^2 & cs & s^2 \end{bmatrix}$ | $c = \cos\theta$, $s = \sin\theta$ |
| 11 | Plane stress constitutive matrix | $\mathbf{D} = \dfrac{E}{1-\nu^2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \frac{1-\nu}{2} \end{bmatrix}$ | Thin loaded in-plane |
| 12 | Plane strain constitutive matrix | $\mathbf{D} = \dfrac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}\begin{bmatrix} 1-\nu & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1-\nu & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \frac{1-2\nu}{2} \end{bmatrix}$ | Thick constrained in transverse direction |
| 13 | Von Mises equivalent stress (3-D) | $\sigma_{v} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2+(\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2+(\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}{2}}$ | Used for ductile yielding (distortion-energy theory) |
| 14 | Convergence criterion (mesh refinement) | $\dfrac{\vert \delta_{n+1} - \delta_n \vert}{\vert \delta_{n+1} \vert} \leq \text{TOL}$ | Typically TOL = 0.5% to 1% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units in FEA software:** Always use a **consistent unit system**. Mixing mm with N and GPa is the single most common cause of "wrong by a factor of 1000" errors in KTU lab records. Recommended KTU lab unit system: **Length = mm, Force = N, Stress = MPa, Mass = tonne (t)**. This way $E_{steel} = 200\,000 \text{ MPa}$, $E_{aluminium} = 70\,000 \text{ MPa}$, $\rho_{steel} = 7.85 \times 10^{-9} \text{ t/mm}^3$.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Static structural analysis is the **workhorse of mechanical and civil design**. It is used to:

- Validate the design of aircraft wings, automotive chassis, and pressure vessels before any prototype is built.
- Predict failure locations in bridges, dams, and skyscrapers.
- Optimize topology (Topology Optimization) to remove material from low-stress regions, reducing weight in aerospace and automotive applications.
- Compute fatigue life when combined with cyclic loading data.

In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned curriculum, the ability to **validate a numerical solution against a known analytical benchmark** (a "back-of-the-envelope" check) is a direct application of **Course Outcome CO2**: "Apply numerical methods and engineering software for analysis of engineering problems."
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivations are **must-know** for the KTU university practical exam viva voce. The examiner will almost certainly ask: *"How did you validate your FEA result?"* The answer lies in the analytical comparison shown below.

### 3.1 Derivation: Cantilever Beam Tip Deflection Under End Point Load

We will derive the formula $\delta_{tip} = \dfrac{PL^3}{3EI}$ from first principles using the **Euler-Bernoulli beam theory** assumptions:
- Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending.
- The beam material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic.
- Deflections are small compared to the beam length.
- Shear deformation is neglected (valid for slender beams where $L/h \gg 10$).

**Step 1:** Consider a cantilever beam of length $L$, fixed at $x = 0$ and free at $x = L$. A vertical point load $P$ acts downward at the free end.

**Step 2:** Compute the bending moment $M(x)$ at any section located at distance $x$ from the fixed end. Cut the beam at section $x$ and consider the free-body of the right-hand portion (from $x$ to $L$):

$$M(x) = -P(L - x)$$

The negative sign indicates a hogging (sagging-negative) moment consistent with the chosen sign convention. For magnitude calculation, we use $\vert M(x) \vert = P(L - x)$.

**Step 3:** The Euler-Bernoulli equation governing the elastic curve $v(x)$ is:

$$EI\,\dfrac{d^2 v}{dx^2} = M(x) = -P(L - x)$$

**Step 4:** Integrate once to obtain the slope (rotation) of the beam:

$$EI\,\dfrac{dv}{dx} = \int -P(L - x)\,dx = -P\left(Lx - \dfrac{x^2}{2}\right) + C_1$$

**Step 5:** Integrate a second time to obtain the deflection:

$$EI\,v(x) = -P\left(\dfrac{Lx^2}{2} - \dfrac{x^3}{6}\right) + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 6:** Apply the geometric (essential) boundary conditions at the fixed end $x = 0$:

- Zero deflection: $v(0) = 0$
- Zero slope: $v'(0) = 0$

Substituting $x = 0$ in the slope equation gives $C_1 = 0$. Substituting $x = 0$ in the deflection equation gives $C_2 = 0$.

**Step 7:** Write the final elastic curve equation:

$$EI\,v(x) = -P\left(\dfrac{Lx^2}{2} - \dfrac{x^3}{6}\right)$$

**Step 8:** Evaluate the deflection at the free end $x = L$:

$$EI\,v(L) = -P\left(\dfrac{L \cdot L^2}{2} - \dfrac{L^3}{6}\right) = -P\left(\dfrac{L^3}{2} - \dfrac{L^3}{6}\right)$$

$$EI\,v(L) = -P\left(\dfrac{3L^3 - L^3}{6}\right) = -P\left(\dfrac{2L^3}{6}\right) = -\dfrac{PL^3}{3}$$

**Step 9:** The magnitude of the tip deflection is therefore:

$$\boxed{\delta_{tip} = \vert v(L) \vert = \dfrac{PL^3}{3EI}}$$

This is the canonical analytical result that the KTU examiner expects you to use for validating the FEA tip displacement of a cantilever beam.

### 3.2 Derivation: 2-Node Truss Element Stiffness Matrix in Local Coordinates

Consider a 1-D bar of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and Young's modulus $E$, with nodes 1 and 2. The local coordinate $x$ runs from node 1 to node 2.

**Step 1:** Approximate the displacement field within the element using linear shape functions:

$$u(x) = N_1(x)\,u_1 + N_2(x)\,u_2$$

where the linear shape functions are:

$$N_1(x) = 1 - \dfrac{x}{L}, \qquad N_2(x) = \dfrac{x}{L}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the strain (1-D axial strain):

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{du}{dx} = -\dfrac{1}{L}u_1 + \dfrac{1}{L}u_2 = \dfrac{1}{L}\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix}$$

Define the strain-displacement matrix:

$$\mathbf{B} = \dfrac{1}{L}\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Hooke's law for 1-D linear elastic material:

$$\sigma = E\varepsilon = E\,\mathbf{B}\,\mathbf{d}$$

The material matrix is simply $\mathbf{D} = E$ (scalar in 1-D).

**Step 4:** Compute the element stiffness matrix using the standard FEA integral:

$$\mathbf{k}^e = \int_{0}^{L} \mathbf{B}^T \mathbf{D} \mathbf{B}\, A\, dx$$

Substitute $\mathbf{B}$, $\mathbf{D}$, and note that the integrand is constant with respect to $x$:

$$\mathbf{k}^e = \int_{0}^{L} \dfrac{1}{L}\begin{Bmatrix} -1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix} E \dfrac{1}{L}\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} A\, dx = \dfrac{AE}{L^2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\int_{0}^{L} dx$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the integral $\int_{0}^{L} dx = L$:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{k}^e = \dfrac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}$$

This is the **local stiffness matrix** of a 1-D truss (bar) element. For 2-D truss elements, this is rotated into global coordinates using the transformation matrix built from direction cosines $c = \cos\theta$ and $s = \sin\theta$.

### 3.3 Sample Numerical Validation (Worked Example for Lab Record)

**Problem statement (typical KTU Module 2 experiment):**
A steel cantilever beam of length $L = 1 \text{ m}$, rectangular cross-section $b = 25 \text{ mm} \times h = 5 \text{ mm}$, is loaded at its free end with a downward point load $P = 50 \text{ N}$. Compute the analytical tip deflection, then validate against an FEA solver result.

**Given data:**
- $L = 1000 \text{ mm}$
- $b = 25 \text{ mm}$, $h = 5 \text{ mm}$
- $A = b \cdot h = 125 \text{ mm}^2$
- $I = \dfrac{bh^3}{12} = \dfrac{25 \times 5^3}{12} = \dfrac{25 \times 125}{12} = \dfrac{3125}{12} = 260.4167 \text{ mm}^4$
- $E = 200\,000 \text{ MPa} = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- $P = 50 \text{ N}$

**Analytical solution:**

$$\delta_{analytical} = \dfrac{PL^3}{3EI} = \dfrac{50 \times 1000^3}{3 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 260.4167}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$50 \times 10^9 = 5 \times 10^{10}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$3 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 260.4167 = 6 \times 10^5 \times 260.4167 = 1.5625 \times 10^8$$

Divide:

$$\delta_{analytical} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{10}}{1.5625 \times 10^8} = 320 \text{ mm}$$

**FEA result (assume):** $\delta_{FEA} = 318.4 \text{ mm}$ (slightly lower due to shear deformation neglected in analytical formula).

**Percentage error:**

$$\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{\vert \delta_{FEA} - \delta_{analytical} \vert}{\delta_{analytical}} \times 100 = \dfrac{\vert 318.4 - 320 \vert}{320} \times 100 = 0.5\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A percentage error **less than 5%** is generally accepted in KTU lab evaluation. Errors above 5% usually indicate a wrong material assignment, inconsistent units, insufficient mesh refinement, or missing boundary conditions.

### 3.4 Mesh Convergence Procedure (Mandatory Step for Full Marks)

To demonstrate that your FEA solution is **grid-independent**, repeat the analysis with progressively finer meshes and tabulate the result:

| Mesh Level | Number of Elements | Tip Deflection (mm) | % Change from Previous |
|------------|--------------------|---------------------|-------------------------|
| Coarse | 10 | 305.0 | — |
| Medium | 50 | 316.2 | 3.67 |
| Fine | 200 | 318.4 | 0.70 |
| Very fine | 800 | 318.7 | 0.09 |

Convergence is achieved when the % change between successive refinements drops below your chosen tolerance (typically **1%**). Always include this table in your KTU lab record.
