## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions are calibrated for the KTU 2024 Scheme **Continuous Evaluation (CE)** and **End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern. For lab courses like **PCAUL508**, marks are split between the **lab record (40%)**, **viva voce (20%)**, **practical examination (40%)**. The questions below test both the practical execution and underlying theory.

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "Finite Element Method" and list the three main steps involved in static structural analysis using FEA.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Finite Element Method (FEM) is a numerical technique for solving boundary value problems in engineering by discretizing a continuous domain into a finite number of smaller sub-domains called elements. The three main steps are:
1. **Pre-processing:** Geometry creation, material assignment, element type selection, and mesh generation.
2. **Solving:** Assembly of global stiffness matrix, application of boundary conditions and loads, and solution of the equilibrium equation $\mathbf{K}\mathbf{U} = \mathbf{F}$.
3. **Post-processing:** Extraction and visualization of nodal displacements, strains, stresses, and validation against analytical results.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three steps in correct order.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*What is the significance of mesh convergence study in FEA? How is it performed?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Mesh convergence study is performed to ensure that the FEA solution is **independent of the mesh density** — i.e., further mesh refinement does not significantly change the result. It is performed by:
1. Running the same analysis with progressively finer meshes (e.g., coarse, medium, fine).
2. Recording the quantity of interest (e.g., tip deflection, max stress) for each mesh.
3. Computing the percentage change between successive mesh refinements.
4. Declaring convergence when the percentage change drops below a chosen tolerance (typically **1% to 5%**).

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for procedure.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Cantilever Beam Analysis

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
A mild steel cantilever beam has length $L = 2 \text{ m}$, rectangular cross-section of width $b = 40 \text{ mm}$ and height $h = 10 \text{ mm}$. A point load $P = 100 \text{ N}$ is applied vertically downward at the free end. The Young's modulus is $E = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ MPa}$ and Poisson's ratio is $\nu = 0.3$.

**(a)** Compute the analytical tip deflection and maximum bending stress using classical Euler-Bernoulli beam theory. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Set up the problem for FEA analysis: list the geometry, material, element type, mesh, boundary conditions, and load application steps. Explain how you will validate the FEA result. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part A (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Cross-section properties.**
- Area: $A = b \cdot h = 40 \times 10 = 400 \text{ mm}^2$
- Second moment of area: $I = \dfrac{bh^3}{12} = \dfrac{40 \times 10^3}{12} = \dfrac{40000}{12} = 3333.33 \text{ mm}^4$
- Section modulus: $Z = \dfrac{I}{h/2} = \dfrac{3333.33}{5} = 666.67 \text{ mm}^3$

**Step 2: Tip deflection (Euler-Bernoulli).**
Use the formula $\delta_{tip} = \dfrac{PL^3}{3EI}$.
Convert units: $L = 2000 \text{ mm}$, $P = 100 \text{ N}$, $E = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$, $I = 3333.33 \text{ mm}^4$.

$$\delta_{tip} = \dfrac{100 \times (2000)^3}{3 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 3333.33}$$

Numerator: $100 \times 8 \times 10^9 = 8 \times 10^{11}$.
Denominator: $6 \times 10^5 \times 3333.33 = 2 \times 10^9$.

$$\delta_{tip} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^{11}}{2 \times 10^9} = 400 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Maximum bending moment.**
The maximum bending moment occurs at the fixed end: $M_{max} = P \cdot L = 100 \times 2 = 200 \text{ N·m} = 200\,000 \text{ N·mm}$.

**Step 4: Maximum bending stress.**
$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{Z} = \dfrac{200\,000}{666.67} = 300 \text{ MPa}$$

---

**Model Solution for Part A (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Geometry.** Create a 3-D solid body: rectangular box with $L = 2000 \text{ mm}$, $b = 40 \text{ mm}$, $h = 10 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 2: Material.** Structural Steel: $E = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ MPa}$, $\nu = 0.3$, $\rho = 7.85 \times 10^{-9} \text{ t/mm}^3$.

**Step 3: Element type.** For 3-D solid: **SOLID185** (8-node brick) or **SOLID187** (10-node tetrahedral). For 1-D idealization: **BEAM188**.

**Step 4: Mesh.** Use a fine mesh with element edge size $\le 10 \text{ mm}$. Apply mesh refinement near the fixed end where stress gradients are high.

**Step 5: Boundary conditions.** At the fixed end face ($x = 0$), constrain all six DOFs: $U_x = U_y = U_z = 0$ and $\theta_x = \theta_y = \theta_z = 0$ (or simply all displacements for a solid element).

**Step 6: Load.** Apply $P = 100 \text{ N}$ in the negative $Y$ direction at the free end face (or at the centroid of the free end).

**Step 7: Solve and validate.**
- Run the static analysis solver.
- Extract tip deflection at $x = L$ in the $Y$ direction and maximum von Mises stress at the fixed end.
- Compare with analytical values: $\delta_{analytical} = 400 \text{ mm}$ and $\sigma_{analytical} = 300 \text{ MPa}$.
- Compute percentage error:
$$\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{\vert \delta_{FEA} - \delta_{analytical} \vert}{\delta_{analytical}} \times 100$$
- A mesh convergence study should be reported (e.g., 50, 500, 5000 elements) showing the deflection stabilizing near 400 mm.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Truss Analysis (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - June 2023]**
A 2-D pin-jointed truss consists of three bars forming an equilateral triangle of side $a = 1 \text{ m}$. Bars 1 and 2 are inclined at $\pm 60°$ to the horizontal, and bar 3 is the horizontal base. All bars are made of steel with $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$ and $A = 100 \text{ mm}^2$. A vertical downward load $P = 5 \text{ kN}$ is applied at the apex node.

**(a)** Derive the global stiffness matrix for one inclined bar in global coordinates and assemble the system. Solve for nodal displacements at the loaded node. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the member forces analytically using the method of joints and validate against the FEA result. Discuss the role of element type selection in this problem. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part B (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Geometry and connectivity.**
- Node 1 (apex) at $(0, h)$ where $h = a \sin 60° = 1 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 0.866 \text{ m}$.
- Node 2 (right support) at $(a/2, 0) = (0.5, 0)$.
- Node 3 (left support) at $(-0.5, 0)$.

Bar 1 connects Node 1 to Node 2; Bar 2 connects Node 1 to Node 3. Bar 3 connects Node 2 to Node 3 (base).

**Step 2: Element stiffness matrix for bar 1 (inclined at $-60°$).**
- Length: $L_1 = a = 1 \text{ m} = 1000 \text{ mm}$.
- Direction cosines: $c = \cos(-60°) = 0.5$, $s = \sin(-60°) = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = -0.866$.

$$\mathbf{k}_1 = \dfrac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} c^2 & cs & -c^2 & -cs \\ cs & s^2 & -cs & -s^2 \\ -c^2 & -cs & c^2 & cs \\ -cs & -s^2 & cs & s^2 \end{bmatrix} = \dfrac{100 \times 200\,000}{1000}\begin{bmatrix} 0.25 & -0.433 & -0.25 & 0.433 \\ -0.433 & 0.75 & 0.433 & -0.75 \\ -0.25 & 0.433 & 0.25 & -0.433 \\ 0.433 & -0.75 & -0.433 & 0.75 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{k}_1 = 20\,000\begin{bmatrix} 0.25 & -0.433 & -0.25 & 0.433 \\ -0.433 & 0.75 & 0.433 & -0.75 \\ -0.25 & 0.433 & 0.25 & -0.433 \\ 0.433 & -0.75 & -0.433 & 0.75 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N/mm}$$

**Step 3: Bar 2 stiffness (by symmetry).** Bar 2 has the same stiffness matrix (inclined at $+60°$ gives the same $c^2$, $s^2$, $cs$ magnitudes).

**Step 4: Assembly and boundary conditions.** Apply $U_x = U_y = 0$ at Nodes 2 and 3. The reduced global system at Node 1 has DOFs $u_1$ and $v_1$:

$$\mathbf{K}_{red} = 2 \times 20\,000 \begin{bmatrix} 0.25 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.75 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10\,000 & 0 \\ 0 & 30\,000 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N/mm}$$

(The off-diagonal terms cancel by symmetry.)

**Step 5: Solve.** Load vector: $F_y = -5000 \text{ N}$ at Node 1, $F_x = 0$.

$$u_1 = \dfrac{0}{10\,000} = 0$$

$$v_1 = \dfrac{-5000}{30\,000} = -0.1667 \text{ mm}$$

The apex moves down by **0.1667 mm** and has zero horizontal displacement.

---

**Model Solution for Part B (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Method of joints at the apex (Node 1).**
By symmetry, the horizontal components of the forces in the two inclined bars cancel, and the vertical components support the load $P$. Let $F_1$ and $F_2$ be the axial forces in bars 1 and 2 (tension positive).

Equilibrium in the $Y$ direction:
$$F_1 \sin 60° + F_2 \sin 60° = P$$

By symmetry, $F_1 = F_2 = F$. Therefore:
$$2F \sin 60° = P \implies F = \dfrac{P}{2 \sin 60°} = \dfrac{5000}{2 \times 0.866} = 2886.75 \text{ N (tension)}$$

**Step 2: Compute the elongation of each inclined bar.**
$$\delta_1 = \dfrac{F_1 L_1}{A E} = \dfrac{2886.75 \times 1000}{100 \times 200\,000} = 0.1443 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Relate bar elongation to apex displacement.**
The vertical displacement of the apex equals the elongation of the bar times $\sin 60°$ (small deformation assumption):
$$v_1 = -\dfrac{\delta_1}{\sin 60°} = -\dfrac{0.1443}{0.866} = -0.1667 \text{ mm}$$

This matches the FEA result from part (a) exactly. Percentage error: **0%** (analytical and FEA are mathematically equivalent for a linear truss).

**Step 4: Role of element type.** The truss problem is best modeled using **1-D line elements** (LINK1 in ANSYS, T2D2 in ABAQUS) because the bars carry only axial loads and the assumption of pin joints eliminates bending moments. Using 2-D plane stress or 3-D solid elements for such a problem would (a) be computationally expensive, (b) introduce parasitic bending stresses, and (c) require careful mesh refinement at the joints to capture the stress concentration. The 1-D element yields a closed-form match with hand calculations, which is the gold standard for FEA validation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (most common, –3 to –4 marks):** Mixing SI prefixes (e.g., mm with N and GPa) leads to errors of order $10^3$. Always declare your unit system at the top of your lab record.
> 2. **Missing Mesh Convergence (–2 marks):** Simply reporting one FEA result without demonstrating grid-independence will lose marks. Always run at least three mesh refinements.
> 3. **Wrong Element Type (–2 marks):** Using a beam element for a truss or a solid element for a thin plate without proper aspect ratio is a serious error. Match element type to physics.
> 4. **No Analytical Validation (–4 marks):** FEA results without analytical comparison are treated as incomplete lab work. The percentage error table is **mandatory**.
> 5. **Forgetting to Apply Self-Weight (–1 mark):** If the problem statement implies gravitational loading, remember to set the acceleration of gravity in the correct direction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> The following bullets summarize the **absolute essentials** for the KTU 2024 ESE practical exam and viva voce on **Module 2: Structural Analysis**.

**Foundational Concepts**
- Structural analysis in the CAD/Analysis lab context = **FEA-based static analysis** of beams, trusses, frames, or plates.
- The governing equation is the **global equilibrium**: $\mathbf{K}\mathbf{U} = \mathbf{F}$.
- FEA workflow = **pre-processing → solving → post-processing**, plus validation.
- Two unknowns to extract: **displacement (deflection)** and **stress (von Mises or principal)**.

**Analytical Benchmarks (must memorize)**
- Cantilever beam tip deflection under end point load: $\delta = \dfrac{PL^3}{3EI}$.
- Cantilever beam tip deflection under UDL: $\delta = \dfrac{wL^4}{8EI}$.
- Simply supported beam centre deflection under central point load: $\delta = \dfrac{PL^3}{48EI}$.
- Simply supported beam centre deflection under UDL: $\delta = \dfrac{5wL^4}{384EI}$.
- Truss bar stiffness in global frame: $\mathbf{k} = \dfrac{AE}{L}\begin{bmatrix} c^2 & cs & -c^2 & -cs \\ cs & s^2 & -cs & -s^2 \\ -c^2 & -cs & c^2 & cs \\ -cs & -s^2 & cs & s^2 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Section Properties (must memorize)**
- Rectangle ($b \times h$): $I = \dfrac{bh^3}{12}$.
- Solid circle (diameter $d$): $I = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{64}$.
- Hollow circle (outer $d_o$, inner $d_i$): $I = \dfrac{\pi (d_o^4 - d_i^4)}{64}$.

**Material Properties (standard values)**
- Structural Steel: $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$, $\nu = 0.3$, $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
- Aluminium Alloy 6061: $E = 70 \text{ GPa}$, $\nu = 0.33$, $\rho = 2700 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
- Cast Iron: $E = 100 \text{ GPa}$, $\nu = 0.25$, $\rho = 7200 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**Software-Specific Pointers (ANSYS Workbench default)**
- 3-D solid element: **SOLID186** (20-node brick, higher order) or **SOLID185** (8-node brick).
- 2-D plane stress/strain: **PLANE183** (8-node quad) or **PLANE182** (4-node quad).
- Beam element: **BEAM188** (3-D, 2-node Timoshenko).
- Truss element: **LINK180** (3-D, 2-node).
- Shell element: **SHELL281** (8-node).

**FEA Best Practices**
- Always perform a **mesh convergence study** before accepting a result.
- Apply **singularity-free** boundary conditions (use cylindrical supports for axes of symmetry).
- For thin members ($L/h > 10$), use beam/shell elements. For thick members ($L/h < 5$), use solid elements.
- Use **sub-stepping** in nonlinear static analysis to aid convergence.
- Report results in **deformed shape + contour + tabulated values**.

**Validation Protocol**
- Compute analytical result first (SOM theory).
- Run FEA with progressively finer mesh.
- Tabulate deflection, stress, and percentage error.
- Acceptance criterion: **error < 5%** at the converged mesh.

**Common Viva Voce Questions**
1. *Why does the FEA tip deflection differ slightly from the analytical Euler-Bernoulli result?* — Because FEA includes shear deformation (Timoshenko effect), while Euler-Bernoulli neglects it.
2. *What is the difference between plane stress and plane strain?* — Plane stress assumes zero out-of-plane stress ($\sigma_z = 0$), used for thin loaded plates. Plane strain assumes zero out-of-plane strain ($\varepsilon_z = 0$), used for thick constrained structures.
3. *What is Saint-Venant's principle and how does it affect your boundary conditions?* — Stress distributions near load/BC application points are non-uniform but equalize far from the point. Always keep BCs and loads far from regions of interest.
4. *Why is the global stiffness matrix sparse?* — Each node connects to only a few neighboring elements, so most off-diagonal entries are zero.
5. *What does a negative eigenvalue in the stiffness matrix indicate?* — A kinematic mechanism (insufficient constraints), i.e., rigid body motion is not suppressed.

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Thermal Analysis** is a numerical simulation technique used in Computer-Aided Engineering (CAE) to determine the temperature distribution and thermal response of a physical system or component when subjected to thermal loads. In the context of the Finite Element Method (FEM), it solves the partial differential equation governing heat conduction (Fourier's Law) over a discretized domain to predict temperature fields, heat fluxes, thermal gradients, and thermal stresses.

Mathematically, thermal analysis determines the spatial and temporal variation of the scalar field $T(x,y,z,t)$ that satisfies the energy balance equation within a domain $\Omega$, subject to prescribed boundary conditions on $\partial\Omega$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Focus:** This experiment focuses on the **steady-state and transient heat conduction analysis** of simple geometries (e.g., a 2D plate, a fin, or a composite wall) using the FEM workflow — typically executed in ANSYS Workbench, ANSYS Mechanical APDL, or SOLIDWORKS Simulation.

## 1.2 Types of Thermal Analysis (Syllabus Classification)

| Analysis Type | Governing Behavior | Time Dependence | Primary Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Steady-State Thermal** | $\partial T / \partial t = 0$ | None | Final temperature field |
| **Transient Thermal** | $\partial T / \partial t \neq 0$ | Explicit time integration | Temperature vs. time history |
| **Thermal-Stress (Coupled)** | Thermal strain coupling | Often quasi-static | Displacement/stress from $\Delta T$ |
| **Conjugate Heat Transfer (CHT)** | Conduction + Convection + Radiation | May be steady/transient | Combined solid-fluid T-field |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Electrical Network" Picture

Imagine a copper plate with one edge placed on a hot stove and the opposite edge exposed to cool air. Heat will flow from the hot side to the cold side. Now visualize this system as an **electrical circuit**:

- **Temperature ($T$)** is analogous to **Voltage ($V$)**
- **Heat Flow Rate ($q$)** is analogous to **Electric Current ($I$)**
- **Thermal Resistance ($R_{th}$)** is analogous to **Electrical Resistance ($R$)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Insight:** Just as Ohm's Law states $V = IR$, Fourier's Law states $q = -kA(dT/dx)$. A high conductivity ($k$ large) means heat flows *easily* — like a thick copper wire carrying current effortlessly. An insulator (low $k$) *resists* heat flow, analogous to a high-resistance resistor.

This analogy is powerful because the matrix equations solved by the FEM thermal solver (the **stiffness matrix** [C] or **conductance matrix**) have the *exact same structure* as the nodal analysis equations solved in an electrical circuit. This is why thermal FE problems are often among the *easiest* to understand — they mirror familiar DC circuit theory.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.670374419 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- **Typical thermal conductivity ranges:**
  - **Pure Copper (annealed):** $k \approx \mathbf{401 \text{ W/m·K}}$
  - **AISI 304 Stainless Steel:** $k \approx \mathbf{16.2 \text{ W/m·K}}$
  - **Air at 300 K (still):** $k \approx \mathbf{0.0262 \text{ W/m·K}}$
  - **Fiberglass Insulation:** $k \approx \mathbf{0.04 \text{ W/m·K}}$
- **Standard reference temperature:** $T_{ref} = 20^\circ C = 293.15 \text{ K}$ (for thermal stress coupling)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examination Tip:** Students are expected to know the **units** of all thermal quantities by heart. Memorize: $k$ in W/(m·K), $h$ (convection coefficient) in W/(m²·K), $\alpha$ (diffusivity) in m²/s, and $q$ (flux) in W/m².

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Governing Equation — Heat Diffusion Equation

The foundation of all thermal FEM analysis is the **general heat conduction (diffusion) equation**, derived from the conservation of energy in a differential control volume:

$$\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(k_x \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(k_y \frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(k_z \frac{\partial T}{\partial z}\right) + \dot{Q}_{gen}$$

**Symbol-by-Symbol Meaning:**
- $\rho$ = material density $\left[\text{kg/m}^3\right]$
- $c_p$ = specific heat capacity at constant pressure $\left[\text{J/kg·K}\right]$
- $T$ = temperature field $\left[\text{K or } ^\circ C\right]$
- $t$ = time $\left[\text{s}\right]$
- $k_x, k_y, k_z$ = directional thermal conductivities $\left[\text{W/m·K}\right]$
- $\dot{Q}_{gen}$ = internal heat generation rate per unit volume $\left[\text{W/m}^3\right]$

## 2.2 Reduction to Common Practical Cases

For an **isotropic** material ($k_x = k_y = k_z = k$), the equation simplifies to:

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \nabla^2 T + \frac{\dot{Q}_{gen}}{\rho c_p}$$

where $\alpha = k / (\rho c_p)$ is the **thermal diffusivity** $\left[\text{m}^2/\text{s}\right]$.

For **steady-state** ($\partial T / \partial t = 0$) with **no internal heat generation** ($\dot{Q}_{gen} = 0$):

$$\nabla^2 T = 0 \quad \text{(Laplace's Equation)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Steady-state analysis with no generation collapses to Laplace's equation. The FEM discretization of Laplace's equation is a direct analog of the 1D/2D Poisson/Laplace problems taught in mathematics, making it the most common board-examination question type.

## 2.3 The Three Pillars — Boundary Conditions

The PDE above is **not solvable** in isolation. It must be supplemented by **boundary conditions (BCs)** on $\partial\Omega$:

### (a) Dirichlet (Prescribed Temperature / Essential BC)
$$T(x,y,z,t) = T_{prescribed} \quad \text{on } \Gamma_1$$
*Example:* A surface held at 100°C by immersion in boiling water.

### (b) Neumann (Prescribed Heat Flux / Natural BC)
$$-k \frac{\partial T}{\partial n} = q_n \quad \text{on } \Gamma_2$$
*Example:* A surface receiving solar radiation of 800 W/m².

### (c) Robin / Cauchy (Convection / Mixed BC)
$$-k \frac{\partial T}{\partial n} = h(T_s - T_\infty) \quad \text{on } \Gamma_3$$
*Example:* A surface cooling in moving air.

Special case of (c) is **insulated (adiabatic)**: $h = 0$ or $q_n = 0$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **complete cheat-sheet** for the Thermal Analysis module. Print it and keep it next to you during problem-solving.

| # | Equation / Law | Statement | Typical Use in KTU Problems |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fourier's Law (1D)** | $q_x = -k \, dT/dx$ | Heat flux through a wall |
| 2 | **Heat Diffusion (General)** | $\rho c_p \partial T/\partial t = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \dot{Q}$ | Governing PDE |
| 3 | **Steady Laplace (No Source)** | $\nabla^2 T = 0$ | Simplified solver form |
| 4 | **Thermal Diffusivity** | $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ | Transient time-scale analysis |
| 5 | **Convection BC** | $q = h(T_s - T_\infty)$ | Heat loss to fluid |
| 6 | **Radiation BC** | $q = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$ | High-T applications |
| 7 | **Thermal Resistance (Conduction)** | $R_{cond} = L/(kA)$ | Network analogy |
| 8 | **Thermal Resistance (Convection)** | $R_{conv} = 1/(hA)$ | Network analogy |
| 9 | **Overall Heat Transfer (Composite)** | $U = 1/\sum R_i$ | Multi-layer wall problems |
| 10 | **FEM Element Equation** | $[K]\{T\} = \{Q\}$ | Matrix form of discretized system |
| 11 | **Element Conductance (1D Bar)** | $K_e = (kA/L) \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Manual FEM hand calculation |
| 12 | **Heat Generation Element (1D)** | $Q_e = (q A L/6) \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | With source term |

| 14 | **Fourier Number** | $Fo = \alpha t / L_c^2$ | Transient progress measure |
| 15 | **Lumped Capacitance Solution** | $(T-T_\infty)/(T_0-T_\infty) = e^{-(hA/(\rho V c_p))t}$ | $Bi < 0.1$ approximation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Anchor for KTU:** Equation 10 ($[K]\{T\} = \{Q\}$) is the **MOST IMPORTANT** formula. It is the matrix form of the FE solution. Students must be able to write this for a 1D two-node bar element, 2D triangular element, and identify what each term represents physically.

## 2.5 The FEM Workflow — Operational Logic

The standard steps executed by any commercial thermal solver (ANSYS, Abaqus, COMSOL) are:

1. **Pre-processing:** Geometry creation, material assignment ($k$, $\rho$, $c_p$), mesh generation, BC application
2. **Element Formulation:** Construction of the element conductance matrix $[k_e]$ and consistent load vector $\{f_e\}$ via the Galerkin weighted-residual method
3. **Assembly:** Global matrix $[K]$ and vector $\{F\}$ assembled by summation over all elements
4. **Application of BCs:** Essential BCs applied by row/column elimination or penalty method
5. **Solution:** Direct (Gaussian elimination) or iterative (CG, GMRES) solver applied to $[K]\{T\} = \{F\}$
6. **Post-processing:** Contour plots, nodal temperature extraction, heat flux calculation, reaction heat flux

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fourier's Law from Physical First Principles

**Goal:** Show rigorously that $q_x = -k \, dT/dx$.

**Step 1 — Definition of Heat Flux:**
Heat flux $q_x$ is defined as the amount of thermal energy $Q$ crossing a unit area $A$ per unit time $t$, in the $x$-direction:

$$q_x = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{1}{\Delta A} \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta t} \quad \left[\text{W/m}^2\right]$$

**Step 2 — Empirical Observation (Fourier, 1822):**
Experimentally, Joseph Fourier observed that the rate of heat transfer is *proportional* to the spatial temperature gradient. The minus sign appears because heat flows from hot to cold (i.e., in the direction of *decreasing* temperature, so if $dT/dx > 0$, then $q_x$ must be negative):

$$q_x \propto -\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}$$

**Step 3 — Introduction of Material Property:**
The constant of proportionality is the **thermal conductivity** $k$ (a positive material property for passive media):

$$q_x = -k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}$$

**Step 4 — Generalization to 3D (Vector Form):**
The total heat flux vector $\vec{q}$ in 3D isotropic media is:

$$\vec{q} = -k \nabla T$$

**Physical Interpretation:** This single equation encodes the entire empirical behavior of heat conduction. The negative sign is a statement of the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** — heat spontaneously flows down a temperature gradient.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Heat Conduction Equation from Energy Conservation

**Goal:** Derive $\rho c_p (\partial T/\partial t) = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \dot{Q}$ from first principles.

**Step 1 — Consider an Infinitesimal Control Volume:**
Take a small rectangular parallelepiped of dimensions $dx \times dy \times dz$ fixed in space. Energy within this element is $E = \rho c_p T \, dx \, dy \, dz$.

**Step 2 — Rate of Energy Accumulation:**
$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial t} = \rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} \, dx \, dy \, dz$$

**Step 3 — Net Heat Inflow by Conduction (x-direction):**
Heat entering the $x$-face (at $x$): $q_x(x) \, dy \, dz$
Heat leaving the $x$-face (at $x+dx$): $q_x(x+dx) \, dy \, dz$
Net x-inflow: $[q_x(x) - q_x(x+dx)] \, dy \, dz = -(\partial q_x/\partial x) \, dx \, dy \, dz$

Applying **Fourier's Law** $q_x = -k(\partial T/\partial x)$:
$$-\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right) dx\,dy\,dz = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right) dx\,dy\,dz$$

**Step 4 — Repeat for y and z Directions:**
Adding all three conduction contributions:

$$\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial z}\right)\right] dx\,dy\,dz$$

**Step 5 — Add Internal Heat Generation:**
Let $\dot{Q}$ be the volumetric heat source $\left[\text{W/m}^3\right]$:
$$\dot{Q} \, dx \, dy \, dz$$

**Step 6 — Apply Energy Conservation (First Law):**
Rate of accumulation = Net inflow + Generation:

$$\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} dx\,dy\,dz = \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial z}\right)\right] dx\,dy\,dz + \dot{Q} \, dx\,dy\,dz$$

**Step 7 — Cancel the Common Volume Element $dx\,dy\,dz$:**

$$\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial z}\right) + \dot{Q}$$

**Q.E.D.**

For constant $k$ (homogeneous), this simplifies to:

$$\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = k \nabla^2 T + \dot{Q}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — 1D Two-Node Bar Element (Galerkin FEM)

**Goal:** Derive the element matrices for a 1D bar of length $L$, area $A$, conductivity $k$, with two end nodes 1 and 2.

**Step 1 — Governing ODE (1D Steady, No Source):**
$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(k \frac{dT}{dx}\right) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Galerkin Weighted Residual:**
Multiply by a weight function $w(x)$ and integrate over $[0, L]$. Apply integration by parts:

$$\int_0^L k \frac{dw}{dx} \frac{dT}{dx} \, dx = \left[ w k \frac{dT}{dx} \right]_0^L$$

**Step 3 — Approximate the Temperature Field (Linear Shape Functions):**
Within the element, assume $T(x) = N_1 T_1 + N_2 T_2$ where $N_1 = 1 - x/L$ and $N_2 = x/L$.

Equivalently, $\{T\} = [N]\{T_e\}$ where $[N] = \begin{bmatrix} N_1 & N_2 \end{bmatrix}$ and $\{T_e\} = \begin{Bmatrix} T_1 \\ T_2 \end{Bmatrix}$.

**Step 4 — Compute the Derivatives:**
$$\frac{dN_1}{dx} = -\frac{1}{L}, \quad \frac{dN_2}{dx} = \frac{1}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Construct the Element Conductance Matrix $K_e$:**
Substituting the shape functions into the Galerkin integral:

$$K_e = \int_0^L k [B]^T [B] A \, dx$$

where $[B] = \begin{bmatrix} dN_1/dx & dN_2/dx \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1/L & 1/L \end{bmatrix}$

Compute $[B]^T [B]$:

$$[B]^T[B] = \begin{bmatrix} 1/L^2 & -1/L^2 \\ -1/L^2 & 1/L^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Integrate over element length (constant integrand):

$$\int_0^L [B]^T[B] \, dx = \frac{1}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Multiply by $k$ and $A$:

$$\boxed{K_e = \frac{kA}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Step 6 — With Internal Heat Generation $\dot{Q}$ (W/m³):**
The consistent load vector is:

$$F_e = \int_0^L \dot{Q} A [N]^T \, dx = \frac{\dot{Q} A L}{2}\begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 — Final Element Equation:**

$$\frac{kA}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} T_1 \\ T_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} Q_1 \\ Q_2 \end{Bmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This boxed equation appears directly in KTU board exam questions** as a 7-mark derivation. Memorize the structure: the off-diagonals are negative, the matrix is symmetric, and the sum of each row is zero (which has a deep physical meaning — it reflects the absence of internal generation in this simple case).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Lumped Capacitance Solution

**Goal:** Show how transient problems reduce to a simple ODE when $Bi < 0.1$.

**Step 1 — Energy Balance on the Body:**
Net rate of energy loss by convection = Rate of decrease of internal energy:

$$-h A_s (T - T_\infty) = \rho V c_p \frac{dT}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Separate Variables:**
$$\frac{dT}{T - T_\infty} = -\frac{h A_s}{\rho V c_p} dt$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from initial state $(t=0, T=T_0)$ to $(t, T)$:**
$$\int_{T_0}^{T} \frac{dT}{T - T_\infty} = -\frac{h A_s}{\rho V c_p} \int_0^t dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate Both Integrals:**
Left side: $\ln[(T - T_\infty)/(T_0 - T_\infty)]$
Right side: $-(h A_s / \rho V c_p) \, t$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate Both Sides:**

$$\boxed{\frac{T(t) - T_\infty}{T_0 - T_\infty} = \exp\left(-\frac{h A_s}{\rho V c_p} t\right)}$$

**Step 6 — Define Time Constant:**
$$\tau = \frac{\rho V c_p}{h A_s} \quad [\text{s}]$$

This is the **thermal time constant** of the body. After $t = \tau$, the temperature has decayed to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of its initial excess over ambient.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]
**State and explain Fourier's Law of heat conduction. What is the physical significance of the negative sign?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Fourier's Law of heat conduction states that the heat flux $q$ in a given direction is proportional to the negative temperature gradient in that direction:

$$q = -k \frac{dT}{dx}$$

where $k$ is the thermal conductivity of the material in W/(m·K). The **negative sign** is crucial and physically significant — it indicates that heat flows in the direction of *decreasing* temperature, consistent with the Second Law of Thermodynamics. Without the negative sign, the law would predict heat flowing from cold to hot, which is impossible spontaneously. The magnitude $k$ represents the material's ability to conduct heat; metals have high $k$ (e.g., copper ~ 401 W/m·K), while insulators have very low $k$ (e.g., fiberglass ~ 0.04 W/m·K).

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]
**Differentiate between Dirichlet, Neumann, and Robin boundary conditions. Give one engineering example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Type | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dirichlet** | $T = T_0$ on $\Gamma_1$ | Temperature prescribed at surface | Surface immersed in melting ice ($T = 0°C$) |
| **Neumann** | $-k(\partial T/\partial n) = q_n$ on $\Gamma_2$ | Heat flux prescribed at surface | Surface receiving constant heat flux from electric heater |
| **Robin** | $-k(\partial T/\partial n) = h(T_s - T_\infty)$ on $\Gamma_3$ | Convection coupling with ambient fluid | Air-cooled fin of an engine cylinder |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]

**A composite wall consists of three layers in series: Layer 1 (Brick) has thickness $L_1 = 0.1$ m and $k_1 = 0.7$ W/m·K; Layer 2 (Insulation) has $L_2 = 0.05$ m and $k_2 = 0.04$ W/m·K; Layer 3 (Plaster) has $L_3 = 0.02$ m and $k_3 = 0.5$ W/m·K. The inner surface temperature is $T_i = 200°C$ and the outer surface experiences convection with $h = 10$ W/m²K and $T_\infty = 30°C$. The wall area is $A = 1 \text{ m}^2$.**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the equation for the element conductance matrix of a 1D two-node bar element using the Galerkin FEM method. Apply this to compute the temperature at the interface between Layer 1 and Layer 2.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compute the outer surface temperature, total heat transfer rate, and the interface temperature between Layer 2 and Layer 3.

---

**Solution to Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Galerkin Derivation of $K_e$:**

The governing ODE for 1D steady conduction is $\frac{d}{dx}\left(k\frac{dT}{dx}\right) = 0$.

Multiplying by weight $w$ and integrating over element $[0, L]$, then integrating by parts:

$$\int_0^L k \frac{dw}{dx} \frac{dT}{dx} dx = \left[w k \frac{dT}{dx}\right]_0^L$$

Approximating $T \approx N_1 T_1 + N_2 T_2$ with $N_1 = 1 - x/L$, $N_2 = x/L$ and choosing Galerkin weights $w_1 = N_1$, $w_2 = N_2$:

$$\int_0^L k \frac{dN_i}{dx} \frac{dN_j}{dx} dx \cdot \{T_j\} = \{Q_i\}$$

Computing $dN_1/dx = -1/L$ and $dN_2/dx = 1/L$:

$$K_e = \frac{kA}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Interface 1-2 Temperature:**

For Layer 1 (Brick): thermal resistance $R_1 = L_1/(k_1 A) = 0.1/(0.7 \times 1) = 0.1429$ K/W.

Apply overall resistance method. The total resistance from inner to outer:

$R_2 = L_2/(k_2 A) = 0.05/0.04 = 1.25$ K/W
$R_3 = L_3/(k_3 A) = 0.02/0.5 = 0.04$ K/W
$R_{conv} = 1/(hA) = 1/10 = 0.1$ K/W

$R_{total} = 0.1429 + 1.25 + 0.04 + 0.1 = 1.5329$ K/W

Heat transfer rate: $q = (T_i - T_\infty)/R_{total} = (200 - 30)/1.5329 = 110.89$ W

Temperature at interface 1-2:
$T_{12} = T_i - q \times R_1 = 200 - 110.89 \times 0.1429 = 200 - 15.85 = \mathbf{184.15°C}$

---

**Solution to Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Outer Surface Temperature:**
$T_o = T_\infty + q \times R_{conv} = 30 + 110.89 \times 0.1 = \mathbf{41.09°C}$

**Step 2 — Interface 2-3 Temperature:**
$T_{23} = T_o + q \times R_3 = 41.09 + 110.89 \times 0.04 = \mathbf{45.52°C}$

Verification: $T_{23} - T_{12} = q \times R_2$? 
$45.52 - 184.15 = -138.63$ vs $110.89 \times 1.25 = 138.61$ ✓ (sign consistent with heat flowing outward)

**Step 3 — Critical Insight — The Insulation Layer's Effect:**
The insulation layer (Layer 2) accounts for $1.25/1.5329 = 81.5\%$ of total resistance despite being only the second thinnest layer. This is because its $k$ is 17.5× lower than brick. **Engineering takeaway:** Insulation thickness has a disproportionate effect on heat loss.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Sample Style]

**(a)** [7 Marks] For a 1D fin of length $L = 0.1$ m, area $A = 0.001$ m², perimeter $P = 0.1$ m, thermal conductivity $k = 200$ W/m·K, convection coefficient $h = 50$ W/m²K, and ambient temperature $T_\infty = 25°C$, with base temperature $T_b = 150°C$ and insulated tip, derive the temperature distribution $T(x)$ analytically and find the tip temperature.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Set up the FEM equation $[K]\{T\} = \{Q\}$ for this fin discretized into **2 equal elements (3 nodes)**. Solve the system to obtain nodal temperatures and compare the FEM tip temperature with the analytical result.

---

**Solution to Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Governing Equation for Fin:**
The 1D fin equation (with convection from sides) is:

$$\frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} - m^2(T - T_\infty) = 0, \quad m^2 = \frac{hP}{kA}$$

**Step 2 — Compute m:**
$m^2 = (50 \times 0.1)/(200 \times 0.001) = 5/0.2 = 25$, so $m = 5 \text{ m}^{-1}$.

**Step 3 — General Solution:**
$T(x) - T_\infty = C_1 e^{mx} + C_2 e^{-mx}$

**Step 4 — Apply BCs:**
At $x=0$: $T(0) = T_b = 150°C$, so $C_1 + C_2 = 125$.
At $x=L$ (insulated tip): $\frac{dT}{dx}\big|_{x=L} = 0$, so $C_1 m e^{mL} - C_2 m e^{-mL} = 0$, giving $C_1 e^{mL} = C_2 e^{-mL}$, so $C_2 = C_1 e^{2mL}$.

**Step 5 — Solve Constants:**
With $mL = 5 \times 0.1 = 0.5$ and $e^{2mL} = e = 2.7183$:
$C_2 = 2.7183 \, C_1$
$C_1 + 2.7183 \, C_1 = 125 \Rightarrow C_1 = 125/3.7183 = 33.62$
$C_2 = 33.62 \times 2.7183 = 91.38$

**Step 6 — Tip Temperature ($x = L$):**
$\theta(L) = C_1 e^{0.5} + C_2 e^{-0.5} = 33.62 \times 1.6487 + 91.38 \times 0.6065 = 55.43 + 55.42 = 110.85$
$\boxed{T(L) = T_\infty + \theta(L) = 25 + 110.85 = \mathbf{135.85°C}}$

---

**Solution to Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Element Length:** $L_e = 0.05$ m, 3 nodes at $x = 0, 0.05, 0.1$ m.

**Step 2 — Element Matrices (with convection contribution):**
For each element, the element matrix becomes (including side convection):

$$K_e + H_e = \frac{kA}{L_e}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \frac{hPL_e}{6}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute values:
$\frac{kA}{L_e} = \frac{200 \times 0.001}{0.05} = 4$ W/K
$\frac{hPL_e}{6} = \frac{50 \times 0.1 \times 0.05}{6} = 0.04167$ W/K

So $K_e + H_e = \begin{bmatrix} 4.083 & -3.917 \\ -3.917 & 4.083 \end{bmatrix}$

**Step 3 — Assemble Global Matrix (3×3):**
Both elements are identical. Summing contributions:

$$[K] = \begin{bmatrix} 4.083 & -3.917 & 0 \\ -3.917 & 8.167 & -3.917 \\ 0 & -3.917 & 4.083 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Load Vector:**
The convection contribution to load is $\{hPT_\infty L_e/6\}\{1, 1, 1\}^T = 0.04167 \times 25 \times \{1, 1, 1\}^T$ for each interior contribution. For symmetric assembly, each interior node receives contributions from two elements:

For nodes 1, 2, 3: load vector from convection $= 0.04167 \times 25 \times \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 1.042 \\ 2.083 \\ 1.042 \end{Bmatrix}$

**Step 5 — Apply Essential BC:** $T_1 = 150°C$.

After applying $T_1 = 150$, the reduced system for nodes 2 and 3 is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 8.167 & -3.917 \\ -3.917 & 4.083 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} T_2 \\ T_3 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 2.083 + 3.917 \times 150 \\ 1.042 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 589.63 \\ 1.042 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Solve 2×2 System:**
From row 2: $-3.917 T_2 + 4.083 T_3 = 1.042 \Rightarrow T_2 = (4.083 T_3 - 1.042)/3.917$

Substituting into row 1:
$8.167 \times (4.083 T_3 - 1.042)/3.917 - 3.917 T_3 = 589.63$
$2.085 \times (4.083 T_3 - 1.042) - 3.917 T_3 = 589.63$
$8.513 T_3 - 2.173 - 3.917 T_3 = 589.63$
$4.596 T_3 = 591.80$
$T_3 = \mathbf{128.77°C}$
$T_2 = (4.083 \times 128.77 - 1.042)/3.917 = (525.77 - 1.042)/3.917 = 134.04°C$

**Step 7 — Comparison:**
- Analytical tip: $T(L) = 135.85°C$
- FEM tip (2 elements): $T_3 = 128.77°C$
- **Error:** $|128.77 - 135.85|/135.85 = 5.21\%$

This 5% error with just 2 elements is *typical* and demonstrates the trade-off between mesh density and accuracy — increasing to 4 or 8 elements would bring the error below 1%.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — $k$ in W/m·K, $h$ in W/m²K, $T$ in K or °C. Unit mismatch is a 1-mark deduction each time.
> 2. **Sign convention errors** — In $[K]\{T\} = \{Q\}$, the off-diagonals MUST be negative for conduction. A student writing the matrix with all-positive entries loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the convection load term in Part (b) Q5** — Many students apply convection BC only to the surface but forget to add the side-convection contribution $\{hPT_\infty L/2\}$. This causes the FEM tip temperature to deviate significantly from the analytical value.
> 4. **Improper shape-function normalization** — For a non-unit-length element, the shape functions must be $N_1 = (x_2 - x)/L_e$ and $N_2 = (x - x_1)/L_e$. Using $N_1 = 1 - x$ when $L \neq 1$ is a frequent error.
> 5. **Not converting °C to K for radiation problems** — Stefan-Boltzmann uses **absolute Kelvin**. A student using °C in $T^4$ loses 2-3 marks.
> 6. **In transient problems, not checking $Bi < 0.1$** — Before applying lumped capacitance, you MUST verify $Bi = hL_c/k < 0.1$. If not, the lumped solution is invalid.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Thermal Analysis Module**

### Core Concepts to Memorize
- [x] **Fourier's Law:** $q = -k \nabla T$ — negative sign = heat flows from hot to cold
- [x] **Governing PDE:** $\rho c_p \partial T/\partial t = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \dot{Q}$
- [x] **Steady-state, no source** reduces to **Laplace's equation** $\nabla^2 T = 0$
- [x] **Three BCs:** Dirichlet (T specified), Neumann (q specified), Robin (h coupling)
- [x] **FE element equation for 1D bar:** $K_e = (kA/L)\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$
- [x] **Global FE equation:** $[K]\{T\} = \{Q\}$ — the most important matrix form
- [x] **FEM load with source:** $F_e = (\dot{Q}AL/6)\{1, 1\}^T$ (per element)

### Critical Numbers
- [x] **Stefan-Boltzmann:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴
- [x] **Copper k:** 401 W/m·K
- [x] **Air k (300K):** 0.0262 W/m·K
- [x] **Biot number threshold:** $Bi < 0.1$ for lumped capacitance
- [x] **Fourier number:** $Fo = \alpha t / L_c^2$ — measure of transient progress

### Standard Solution Procedure (Always Follow This Order)
1. **Identify** the geometry (1D, 2D, 3D)
2. **List** material properties ($k$, $\rho$, $c_p$)
3. **State** governing equation with simplifications
4. **Apply** boundary conditions explicitly
5. **Discretize** (mesh) the domain
6. **Formulate** element matrices
7. **Assemble** the global system
8. **Solve** for nodal temperatures
9. **Post-process** to get derived quantities (flux, gradients)

### Common Engineering Judgments
- [x] **Finer mesh** → more accuracy, more compute time
- [x] **High conductivity materials** → nearly uniform temperature distribution
- [x] **Insulation effectiveness** = resistance ratio: $R_{insulation}/R_{total}$
- [x] **Transient steady-state** is reached at approximately $t = 4\tau$ where $\tau$ is the time constant
- [x] **Heat generation** problems (e.g., nuclear fuel rods) need volumetric source term
- [x] **Coupled thermal-stress** analyses use $\epsilon_{th} = \alpha \Delta T$ for thermal strain

### Software-Specific Reminders (for Lab Examination)
- [x] In **ANSYS Workbench**: Drag "Steady-State Thermal" from the Analysis Systems toolbox
- [x] In **APDL**: Use `ET,1,PLANE55` (2D 4-node thermal element) or `SOLID70` (3D 8-node)
- [x] In **SOLIDWORKS**: Use the Thermal Study add-in; set BCs as Temperature or Heat Flux or Convection
- [x] **Always** check mesh quality (aspect ratio < 5:1, Jacobian > 0)
- [x] **Always** validate results against analytical solutions for simple cases (e.g., 1D wall, lumped body)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL508 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Flow Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Flow Analysis** in the context of Computer-Aided Design and Analysis Lab refers to the **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)** based simulation of fluid behavior — including liquids and gases — over a domain of interest, by numerically solving the governing equations of motion (continuity, momentum, and energy) using discretization techniques such as the **Finite Volume Method (FVM)**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme lab terminology, the experiment typically involves:
- Importing a 3D CAD geometry into a CAE pre-processor (e.g., **ANSYS Workbench, SolidWorks Flow Simulation, OpenFOAM**).
- Defining the **flow domain**, generating a **computational mesh**, applying **boundary conditions**, and solving the discretized **Navier-Stokes equations**.
- Post-processing the results to extract **velocity vectors, pressure contours, streamlines, vorticity, and turbulence intensity**.

> [!NOTE]
> **CFD = Computer + Fluid + Dynamics.** The computer numerically approximates what nature solves analytically (in only a few ideal cases) and experimentally (in expensive wind tunnels / water channels).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The River Story"

Imagine you are standing beside a fast-flowing river and you want to know the **velocity of water at every point** — on the surface, near the rocks, in the deep middle, and behind a bridge pillar.

- **Experimentally**: You would drop thousands of tiny colored floats and track them with cameras — expensive, time-consuming, and sometimes impossible.
- **Analytically**: You would try to write an exact math equation for every droplet — works for only the simplest ideal rivers.
- **Computationally (Flow Analysis)**: You divide the entire river into millions of tiny 3D boxes (the **mesh**), and a computer solves a small equation in *each* box, then stitches them together. The result is a digital twin of the river showing velocity, pressure, and turbulence everywhere.

The "boxes" are **control volumes (cells)**, and the "stitching" is the **Finite Volume Method**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core metrics students must remember:**
> - **Reynolds Number (Re)** — dimensionless ratio of inertial to viscous forces; governs **laminar vs. turbulent** flow.
> - **Mach Number (Ma)** — ratio of flow speed to the speed of sound; governs **compressibility**.
> - **Dynamic Viscosity (μ)** — Pa·s; resistance of fluid to deformation.
> - **Kinematic Viscosity (ν = μ/ρ)** — m²/s.
> - **Density (ρ)** — kg/m³.
> - **Standard reference**: Air at **15 °C, 1 atm**: ρ ≈ 1.225 kg/m³, μ ≈ 1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s, ν ≈ 1.48 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s.

### 1.4 Classification of Flow Regimes (Foundation for the Experiment)

| Regime | Re Range (internal pipe flow) | Physical Character |
|---|---|---|
| **Laminar** | Re < 2300 | Smooth, parallel streamlines |
| **Transitional** | 2300 ≤ Re ≤ 4000 | Unstable, intermittent bursts |
| **Turbulent** | Re > 4000 | Chaotic eddies, mixing |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Governing Laws of Fluid Motion

Every flow analysis — whether over an aircraft wing or inside a pipe — is governed by three conservation laws:

1. **Conservation of Mass** → **Continuity Equation**.
2. **Conservation of Momentum** (Newton's 2nd Law) → **Navier-Stokes Equations**.
3. **Conservation of Energy** (1st Law of Thermodynamics) → **Energy Equation** (used only for heat-transfer-coupled flows).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of a CFD Experiment (Why & How)

**Step 1 — Geometry Preparation**
- *Why*: CFD solver needs a watertight solid (or void) body.
- *How*: Import the CAD model (STEP/IGES), suppress irrelevant features, enclose the region in a **flow domain** (the imaginary box of air/water around the object).

**Step 2 — Meshing (Discretization)**
- *Why*: The solver cannot handle an infinite continuum; it must solve on a finite set of points.
- *How*: Split the domain into millions of small cells (tetrahedra, hexahedra, prisms, pyramids). Generate **inflation layers** near walls to capture the steep **boundary layer** gradients.

**Step 3 — Physics Setup (Boundary Conditions)**
- *Why*: The PDEs are indeterminate without stating *what happens at the edges*.
- *How*: Specify **Inlet** (velocity/pressure), **Outlet** (pressure/outflow), **Walls** (no-slip + roughness), and **Symmetry/Periodic** planes.

**Step 4 — Solver Configuration**
- Choose: **Pressure-based** (incompressible) vs. **Density-based** (compressible).
- Select: Steady-state vs. Transient.
- Pick turbulence model: **k-ε, k-ω SST, Spalart-Allmaras, LES**.

**Step 5 — Solution & Convergence**
- Iterate until **residuals** drop below 10⁻⁶ (or monitored quantities stabilize).

**Step 6 — Post-Processing**
- Plot **velocity contours**, **streamlines**, **pressure coefficient (Cp)** on surfaces, and compute derived metrics like **drag/lift coefficients**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table. It forms the backbone of every Part-A and Part-B answer.**

| # | Equation / Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reynolds Number (general) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, L}{\mu} = \dfrac{v \, L}{\nu}$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Re for pipe flow | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, D}{\mu}$ | $D$ = pipe diameter |
| 3 | Re for flow over flat plate | $Re_x = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, x}{\mu}$ | $x$ = distance from leading edge |
| 4 | Mach Number | $Ma = \dfrac{v}{a}$, where $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | $\gamma = 1.4$ for air, $R = 287$ J/kg·K |
| 5 | Continuity (incompressible) | $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ | mass conservation |
| 6 | Continuity (general) | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ | compressible form |
| 7 | Navier-Stokes (momentum) | $\rho \left( \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$ | vector form |
| 8 | Pressure Coefficient | $C_p = \dfrac{p - p_{\infty}}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho v_{\infty}^{2}}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Drag Coefficient | $C_d = \dfrac{2 F_d}{\rho v^{2} A}$ | $A$ = frontal area |
| 10 | Lift Coefficient | $C_l = \dfrac{2 F_l}{\rho v^{2} A}$ | $A$ = planform area |
| 11 | Wall Shear Stress | $\tau_w = \mu \left. \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right\vert_{y=0}$ | no-slip condition |
| 12 | Friction Factor (Blasius) | $f = 0.0791 \, Re^{-0.25}$ | valid for $4000 < Re < 10^{5}$ |
| 13 | Hagen-Poiseuille (laminar pipe) | $\Delta p = \dfrac{32 \mu L v}{D^{2}}$ | fully developed |
| 14 | Bernoulli (inviscid) | $p + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v^{2} + \rho g z = \text{const}$ | along a streamline |
| 15 | Y⁺ (wall resolution) | $Y^{+} = \dfrac{y \cdot u_{\tau}}{\nu}$ | first cell height metric; aim 30–300 for k-ω |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace**: Predicting lift/drag on aircraft wings, missile nose cones, parachute inflation.
- **Automotive**: Reducing drag on car bodies, cooling of brake discs, windshield defrosting.
- **Civil/HVAC**: Ventilation in buildings, wind loading on skyscrapers, smoke extraction.
- **Biomedical**: Blood flow through stents, airflow in nasal cavities, drug aerosol delivery.
- **Process Industry**: Mixing tanks, heat exchangers, pump and turbine design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry-standard tools**: **ANSYS Fluent / CFX**, **STAR-CCM+**, **OpenFOAM** (open-source), **SolidWorks Flow Simulation**, **COMSOL Multiphysics**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Continuity Equation from Mass Conservation (Integral Form)

**Starting premise**: Mass can neither be created nor destroyed inside a fixed control volume (CV).

Let a control volume $V$ be bounded by a closed surface $S$ with outward unit normal $\hat{n}$. Let $\rho$ be fluid density and $\vec{V}$ the velocity vector.

**Step 1 — Rate of mass change inside CV**:
The mass inside CV is $\int_{V} \rho \, dV$. Its time rate is:
$$
\frac{d}{dt} \int_{V} \rho \, dV
$$

**Step 2 — Net mass flux leaving through control surface**:
Mass leaving per unit time through element $dS$ is $\rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dS$. Total outward flux:
$$
\oint_{S} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dS
$$

**Step 3 — Apply conservation** (no sources/sinks inside):
$$
\frac{d}{dt} \int_{V} \rho \, dV + \oint_{S} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dS = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Reynolds Transport Theorem** to move the time derivative inside the integral:
$$
\int_{V} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} dV + \oint_{S} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) dS = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Divergence Theorem** to convert surface integral to volume integral:
$$
\oint_{S} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) dS = \int_{V} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \, dV
$$

**Step 6 — Combine**:
$$
\int_{V} \left[ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \right] dV = 0
$$

**Step 7 — Since the CV is arbitrary**, the integrand must vanish pointwise:
$$
\boxed{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0}
$$

**For incompressible flow** (ρ = constant), this reduces to:
$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reynolds Number from Dimensional Analysis (Navier-Stokes Non-Dimensionalization)

**Starting premise**: For steady, incompressible, viscous flow with characteristic length $L$, velocity $U$, pressure scale $p_0$, and density $\rho$, non-dimensionalize the Navier-Stokes equation.

**Step 1 — Define dimensionless variables**:
$$
x^{*} = \frac{x}{L}, \quad y^{*} = \frac{y}{L}, \quad z^{*} = \frac{z}{L}, \quad u^{*} = \frac{u}{U}, \quad v^{*} = \frac{v}{U}, \quad w^{*} = \frac{w}{U}
$$

$$
p^{*} = \frac{p}{\rho U^{2}}, \quad \nabla^{*} = L \nabla
$$

**Step 2 — Write the x-momentum Navier-Stokes equation (steady)**:
$$
\rho \left( u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} \right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \left( \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial z^{2}} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute dimensionless variables** (note: $\partial/\partial x = (1/L) \partial/\partial x^{*}$):
$$
\rho \cdot \frac{U^{2}}{L} \left( u^{*} \frac{\partial u^{*}}{\partial x^{*}} + v^{*} \frac{\partial u^{*}}{\partial y^{*}} + w^{*} \frac{\partial u^{*}}{\partial z^{*}} \right) = -\frac{\rho U^{2}}{L} \frac{\partial p^{*}}{\partial x^{*}} + \mu \cdot \frac{U}{L^{2}} \left( \frac{\partial^{2} u^{*}}{\partial x^{*2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} u^{*}}{\partial y^{*2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} u^{*}}{\partial z^{*2}} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Divide through by $\rho U^{2}/L$**:
$$
u^{*} \frac{\partial u^{*}}{\partial x^{*}} + v^{*} \frac{\partial u^{*}}{\partial y^{*}} + w^{*} \frac{\partial u^{*}}{\partial z^{*}} = -\frac{\partial p^{*}}{\partial x^{*}} + \frac{\mu}{\rho U L} \left( \frac{\partial^{2} u^{*}}{\partial x^{*2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} u^{*}}{\partial y^{*2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} u^{*}}{\partial z^{*2}} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Identify the dimensionless group**:
$$
\boxed{Re = \frac{\rho U L}{\mu} = \frac{U L}{\nu}}
$$

This single number tells us the **ratio of inertial forces (convective term) to viscous forces (diffusive term)**.

- $Re \ll 1$: viscous dominates → creeping / Stokes flow.
- $Re \gg 1$: inertial dominates → turbulent flow with thin boundary layer.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Pipe Flow Reynolds Number

**Problem**: Water (ν = 1.0 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s) flows through a pipe of diameter $D = 0.05$ m at a mean velocity $v = 2$ m/s. Determine the flow regime.

**Solution**:
$$
Re = \frac{v D}{\nu} = \frac{2 \times 0.05}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.1}{10^{-6}} = 1 \times 10^{5}
$$

Since $Re = 10^{5} \gg 4000$, the flow is **fully turbulent**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Turbulence Model

| Model | When to Use | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Spalart-Allmaras** | External aerodynamics, simple geometries | Cheap, robust for attached flows | Poor for separated flows |
| **Standard k-ε** | General-purpose industrial | Stable, fast convergence | Over-predicts separation |
| **k-ω SST** | Adverse pressure gradients, near-wall | Excellent near-wall accuracy | Sensitive to free-stream turbulence |
| **RSM (Reynolds Stress)** | Highly anisotropic flows (swirl, rotation) | Most physics-rich | Very expensive |
| **LES / DES** | Research, unsteady vortex shedding | Resolves eddies directly | Huge mesh + time cost |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CFD Workflow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[StepA: CAD Geometry Import] --> B[StepB: Geometry Cleanup and Flow Domain]
    B --> C[StepC: Meshing - Discretization]
    C --> D[StepD: Physics Selection - Laminar or Turbulent]
    D --> E[StepE: Material Properties - Air Water Oil]
    E --> F[StepF: Boundary Conditions - Inlet Outlet Wall]
    F --> G[StepG: Solver Setup - Pressure or Density Based]
    G --> H[StepH: Run Simulation - Iterate Residuals]
    H --> I[StepI: Convergence Check]
    I --> J{StepJ: Converged?}
    J -- No --> G
    J -- Yes --> K[StepK: Post Processing]
    K --> L[StepL: Velocity Contours]
    K --> M[StepM: Pressure Contours]
    K --> N[StepN: Streamlines and Vectors]
    K --> O[StepO: Derived Coefficients Cd Cl]
    L --> P[StepP: Report and Validation]
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

### 4.2 Boundary Layer Concept (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[StepA: Free Stream Velocity U] --> B[StepB: Inviscid Outer Region]
    B --> C[StepC: Boundary Layer Edge]
    C --> D[StepD: Viscous Sublayer]
    D --> E[StepE: Wall at Y equals Zero]
    A --> F[StepF: Velocity Profile]
    F --> G[StepG: Log Law Region]
    F --> H[StepH: Buffer Layer]
    F --> I[StepI: Linear Sublayer]
    G --> J[StepJ: Turbulent Production]
    H --> J
    I --> K[StepK: Molecular Viscosity Dominant]
```

### 4.3 Solver Algorithm — SIMPLE Loop (Semi-Implicit Method for Pressure-Linked Equations)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[StepA: Initialize Pressure and Velocity Fields] --> B[StepB: Solve Momentum Equations]
    B --> C[StepC: Compute Pressure Correction Equation]
    C --> D[StepD: Update Pressure and Velocities]
    D --> E[StepE: Solve Scalar Transport if any]
    E --> F[StepF: Check Convergence]
    F --> G{StepG: Residuals Below Target?}
    G -- No --> B
    G -- Yes --> H[StepH: Final Solution Ready]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the SIMPLE loop matters for viva**: Almost every commercial pressure-based solver (Fluent, CFX, OpenFOAM `simpleFoam`) uses a variant of this algorithm. If asked "how does your solver converge?", mention: **guess pressure → solve momentum → correct pressure → repeat until continuity is satisfied**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** Define Reynolds number and state its significance in flow analysis.

**Model Answer**:
Reynolds number is the dimensionless ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow, given by
$$
Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu} = \frac{v L}{\nu}
$$
where $v$ is velocity, $L$ is characteristic length, $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity, $\nu$ is kinematic viscosity, and $\rho$ is density.
**Significance**:
- It determines the **flow regime** — laminar (Re < 2300), transitional (2300–4000), or turbulent (Re > 4000) for pipe flow.
- It governs the **similarity** of flows (dynamic similarity between model and prototype).
- It dictates the choice of **turbulence model** in CFD. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** List any four boundary conditions commonly used in CFD flow analysis.

**Model Answer**:
1. **Velocity Inlet** — prescribed velocity vector and turbulence quantities.
2. **Pressure Inlet / Outlet** — prescribed static pressure (used when flow direction may reverse).
3. **Wall** — no-slip condition ($u = v = w = 0$ at wall) with possible roughness.
4. **Symmetry / Periodic** — mimics an infinite domain by copying flow across a plane.
(Other valid: Mass-flow inlet, outflow, far-field, interior). (3 marks)

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]*

**(a)** Derive the **continuity equation** for a three-dimensional, compressible fluid flow in Cartesian coordinates. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

*Step 1 — Consider a fixed elementary control volume* of dimensions $dx, dy, dz$ with the fluid density $\rho(x,y,z,t)$ and velocity components $u, v, w$.

*Step 2 — Net mass flux in x-direction*:
Mass entering face at $x$: $\rho u \, dy \, dz$.
Mass leaving face at $x + dx$: $\left[ \rho u + \dfrac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} dx \right] dy \, dz$.

*Net x-flux leaving*:
$$
\left[ \rho u + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} dx \right] dy\, dz - \rho u \, dy\, dz = \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} dx\, dy\, dz
$$

*Step 3 — Similarly for y and z*:
$$
\text{Net y-flux leaving} = \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} dx\, dy\, dz
$$
$$
\text{Net z-flux leaving} = \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} dx\, dy\, dz
$$

*Step 4 — Total mass leaving per unit time*:
$$
\left[ \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} \right] dx\, dy\, dz
$$

*Step 5 — Rate of mass change inside CV*:
$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} dx\, dy\, dz
$$

*Step 6 — Apply conservation of mass* (Rate of accumulation + Net efflux = 0):
$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} dx\, dy\, dz + \left[ \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} \right] dx\, dy\, dz = 0
$$

*Step 7 — Cancel $dx\, dy\, dz$ and expand the derivatives*:
$$
\boxed{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + u \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial y} + w \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial z} + \rho \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} \right) = 0}
$$

This can be written compactly as:
$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0
$$

For **incompressible** flow (ρ = constant), the first four terms vanish, leaving:
$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0
$$

**(b)** Air at **20 °C** flows over a flat plate of length $L = 1.5$ m at a free-stream velocity of $v = 15$ m/s. Determine the Reynolds number at the trailing edge and identify the flow regime. Take ρ = 1.204 kg/m³ and μ = 1.82 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

*Step 1 — Identify characteristic length* — for flow over a flat plate, the characteristic length is the distance from the leading edge, $x = L = 1.5$ m.

*Step 2 — Compute kinematic viscosity*:
$$
\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{1.82 \times 10^{-5}}{1.204} = 1.512 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}
$$

*Step 3 — Apply the Reynolds number formula*:
$$
Re_L = \frac{v L}{\nu} = \frac{15 \times 1.5}{1.512 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
Re_L = \frac{22.5}{1.512 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.488 \times 10^{6}
$$

*Step 4 — Identify the flow regime*:
- For a flat plate, the critical Reynolds number is $Re_{crit} \approx 5 \times 10^{5}$.
- Since $Re_L = 1.488 \times 10^{6} \gg 5 \times 10^{5}$, the boundary layer is **fully turbulent** over most of the plate.

*Step 5 — Engineering inference* (for marks):
A turbulent boundary layer produces higher skin-friction drag than a laminar one; if drag reduction is a goal, the engineer should consider a **laminar-flow airfoil shape** or a **turbulent tripping strip** placed at the correct $x$ for design control. (7 marks)

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]*

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **step-by-step procedure** for performing a CFD flow analysis using ANSYS Workbench (or equivalent tool). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

A typical CFD analysis proceeds through the following six stages — illustrated in the block diagram below.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Stage1: Geometry - Import CAD model STEP or IGES] --> B1[Stage2: Meshing - Generate computational grid]
    B1 --> C1[Stage3: Setup - Define materials and BCs]
    C1 --> D1[Stage4: Solution - Choose solver and run]
    D1 --> E1[Stage5: Convergence - Monitor residuals]
    E1 --> F1[Stage6: Post Processing - Plots and reports]
```

*Stage 1 — Geometry*: Import the 3D CAD model into **DesignModeler / SpaceClaim**. Create a **flow domain** (a rectangular prism or cylinder enclosing the body) and suppress small features (holes, fillets < mesh size).

*Stage 2 — Meshing*: Open **ANSYS Meshing**. Choose element size (e.g., default physics preference, or inflation layer with 5–10 layers for accurate boundary-layer resolution). Generate a mesh and check **mesh quality metrics**: skewness < 0.85, aspect ratio < 5, orthogonal quality > 0.15.

*Stage 3 — Setup*: Open **Fluent**. Select:
- Solver: **Pressure-based, Steady, 3D**.
- Viscous model: **k-ε Realizable** (or k-ω SST for aerospace).
- Materials: Air (incompressible) or water.
- Boundary conditions: Velocity inlet (15 m/s), Pressure outlet (0 Pa gauge), Wall on the plate.

*Stage 4 — Solution*: Use **SIMPLE** pressure-velocity coupling, **Second-Order Upwind** discretization for momentum and turbulence, and initialize with **Hybrid Initialization**.

*Stage 5 — Convergence*: Run for ~500–1000 iterations. Check that residuals drop below **10⁻⁶** (continuity) and **10⁻³** (k, ε); monitor drag coefficient for stability.

*Stage 6 — Post-Processing*: Create:
- **Velocity magnitude contours** on a symmetry plane.
- **Static pressure contours** on the plate surface.
- **Streamlines** colored by velocity.
- **Vectors** at chosen cross-sections.
- Compute **drag coefficient** and **lift coefficient** from force reports. (7 marks)

**(b)** A pipe of diameter **D = 100 mm** carries oil (ρ = 900 kg/m³, μ = 0.09 Pa·s) at a flow rate of **Q = 10 L/s**. Compute (i) the mean velocity, (ii) the Reynolds number, and (iii) the friction factor using the Blasius equation. Comment on the flow regime. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

*Step 1 — Cross-sectional area*:
$$
A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.1)^{2}}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{2}
$$

*Step 2 — Mean velocity*:
$$
v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{2}} = 1.273\ \text{m/s}
$$

*Step 3 — Kinematic viscosity*:
$$
\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.09}{900} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}
$$

*Step 4 — Reynolds number*:
$$
Re = \frac{v D}{\nu} = \frac{1.273 \times 0.1}{1.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 1273
$$

Since $Re = 1273 < 2300$, the flow is **laminar**.

*Step 5 — Friction factor* (Darcy friction factor, not Fanning):
For laminar flow in a pipe, $f = 64/Re$:
$$
f = \frac{64}{1273} = 0.0503
$$

> **Note**: The Blasius equation $f = 0.0791 Re^{-0.25}$ is valid **only** for turbulent flow ($4000 < Re < 10^{5}$). Since our flow is laminar, we use the analytical formula $f = 64/Re$ instead. **(This is a common viva trap — examiners deliberately test if you know the validity range!)**

*Step 6 — Pressure drop per unit length* (Hagen-Poiseuille):
$$
\frac{\Delta p}{L} = \frac{32 \mu v}{D^{2}} = \frac{32 \times 0.09 \times 1.273}{(0.1)^{2}} = 366.6\ \text{Pa/m}
$$

*Engineering Comment*: For this highly viscous oil flow, the engineer should specify a pump with sufficient head to overcome the ~366 Pa/m pressure gradient and verify that fittings, bends, and valves do not push the local Re above 2300 (which would change the friction law). (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Using Blasius for laminar flow** — the formula $f = 0.0791 Re^{-0.25}$ is **turbulent-only**; using it for $Re < 2300$ costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units of μ** — Pa·s vs. kg/(m·s) is the same, but Poise vs. cP conversions are a classic blunder.
> 3. **Mixing $C_d$ and $C_f$** — drag coefficient uses frontal area; skin-friction coefficient uses wetted area. Do not interchange.
> 4. **No-slip violation in BCs** — at any solid wall, $u = v = w = 0$ must be set. "Free-slip" is reserved for **symmetry planes** only.
> 5. **Skipping mesh-independence study** — in the lab record, a single mesh is not enough; show results for 2–3 mesh densities and justify the chosen one.
> 6. **Not mentioning turbulence model choice** — always justify *why* you picked k-ε vs. k-ω SST.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Summary — Read this the night before the lab viva / exam.**

- ✅ **Flow analysis = CFD**: numerical solution of Navier-Stokes on a discretized mesh.
- ✅ **Three governing equations**: Continuity, Momentum (N-S), Energy.
- ✅ **Reynolds number** is the single most important dimensionless number — determines regime and turbulence model.
- ✅ **Laminar**: Re < 2300 (pipe); **Turbulent**: Re > 4000.
- ✅ **For pipe flow**: $Re = \dfrac{vD}{\nu}$; **for flat plate**: $Re_x = \dfrac{vx}{\nu}$.
- ✅ **Continuity (incompressible)**: $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$.
- ✅ **Navier-Stokes (incompressible)**: $\rho(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V}) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2}\vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$.
- ✅ **Bernoulli's equation** is only valid for **inviscid, steady, incompressible** flow along a streamline.
- ✅ **Boundary conditions** to remember: Velocity Inlet, Pressure Outlet, Wall (no-slip), Symmetry.
- ✅ **No-slip condition** is the most important wall BC: fluid velocity = 0 at solid surface.
- ✅ **Laminar friction factor** = $64/Re$; **Blasius turbulent friction factor** = $0.0791 Re^{-0.25}$ (only for $4000 < Re < 10^{5}$).
- ✅ **Pressure coefficient** $C_p = \dfrac{p - p_{\infty}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_{\infty}^{2}}$ — used on aerofoils.
- ✅ **Drag coefficient** $C_d = \dfrac{2F_d}{\rho v^{2} A}$ — used in vehicle/aerodynamic design.
- ✅ **CFD workflow**: Geometry → Mesh → Setup → Solve → Converge → Post-process.
- ✅ **Mesh quality matters more than mesh quantity** — check skewness, aspect ratio, Y⁺.
- ✅ **Turbulence model quick-pick**: k-ω SST for aerofoils; k-ε Realizable for pipes/ducts; Spalart-Allmaras for simple external flow.
- ✅ **Y⁺ target**: ~1 for resolving the sublayer; 30–300 for wall functions.
- ✅ **Standard air properties (15 °C, 1 atm)**: ρ = 1.225 kg/m³, μ = 1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s, ν = 1.48 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s.
- ✅ **Standard water properties (20 °C)**: ρ = 998 kg/m³, μ = 1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s, ν = 1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s.
- ✅ **Continuity always holds** — even in turbulent flow, mass is conserved; check it as your first convergence criterion.
- ✅ **Always validate CFD with experiments** — at least one published benchmark (e.g., NACA 0012, backward-facing step).
- ✅ **Open-source tool** to mention in viva: **OpenFOAM** (widely used in research and industry).
